incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Hydrogen gas release and explosion
Incident Type: Chemical reaction, hydrogen release, explosion, and fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: XL 10 (methylhydrogensiloxane dimethylsiloxane copolymer), TD 6/12 Blend (ethoxylated tridecyl alcohol surfactant), 10% KOH (potassium hydroxide solution), EM 652 (emulsion product)
Summary: On May 3, 2019, at AB Specialty Silicones in Waukegan, Illinois, an operator pumped an incorrect chemical (10% KOH) into a batch tank containing XL 10 and TD 6/12 Blend. The incompatible chemicals were stored in nearly identical 55-gallon blue drums, differing only in small labels. The reaction between XL 10 (containing silicon hydride bonds) and aqueous KOH produced hydrogen gas, which released into the production building. The hydrogen gas ignited, causing a massive explosion and fire that fatally injured four employees (Byron Biehn, Jeffrey Cummings, Daniel Nicklas, Allen Stevens), destroyed the production building, and forced operational disruptions.
Recommendations:
Amend OSHA PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, including broadening application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals
Revise EPA Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals
Ensure hydrogen gas detection and alarm systems are properly installed, maintained, and configured based on facility application, manufacturer specifications, codes, standards, and industry guidance
Establish a safety management system addressing process safety at AB Specialty Waukegan facility, including elements from CCPS publications
Incorporate CCPS Essential Practices for Managing Chemical Reactivity Hazards into operations

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Mud Pit Discharge
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Base Mud
Summary: On 11 February 2014, an estimated 144 barrels of Synthetic Base Mud was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from the #3 Active Pit dump valve during a mud pit cleaning and transfer operation. The incident resulted from failure to follow the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), improper Lock Out/Tag Out procedures, and inadequate sampling prior to discharge. The #3 Dump Valve was left in the open position and not properly identified during the operation.
Recommendations:
Installation of additional isolation valve in piping layout to provide barrier against accidental discharge
Enhanced procedures for opening and closing overboard discharge valves
Improved Lock Out/Tag Out execution and verification
Proper sampling from correct pit location before discharge authorization

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unauthorized release of Synthetic Based Mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Based Mud (SBM), drilling fluid
Summary: On 4 September 2023 at approximately 13:20, approximately 24 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from Alaminos Canyon (AC) 857, H&P 205 Drilling Rig located on the Shell Perdido Spar. The discharge resulted from obstructions in two valves (knife valve and butterfly valve) on the degasser tank discharge line that prevented proper sealing. The knife valve was obstructed by cuttings and the butterfly valve was blocked by a piece of rubber, both preventing full closure despite visual verification by crew members.
Recommendations:
Crews will fill processing pits with sea water to check for any leaks through containment valves prior to future operations
Install skillet on processing pit master dump valve to prevent unintentional discharge, consistent with active pit master dump valve design

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Tree Valve Leak
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: Mono Ethylene Glycol, seawater
Summary: On 18 March 2022 at 1800 hours, during abandonment operations on Shell's Alaminos Canyon 815 well SA001 from the Helix Q-4000, a pressure test of a five-barrel cement squeeze failed to hold 1000 psi. Investigation revealed the chemical injection tree valve 1 (CIT1) on the subsea tree was leaking approximately 188 barrels of a 50/50 mixture of Mono Ethylene Glycol and seawater into the Gulf of Mexico. The CIT1 valve had not been functioned to verify closure prior to the pressure test; operators relied on documented position from well handover procedures. Manual ROV intervention and hydraulic functioning of the valve eventually stopped the leak.
Recommendations:
Shell to modify Shell Perdido SA001 Well Handover Procedure to include procedures for verifying status of all valves on the subsea tree

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Hydrogen Sulfide Release
Incident Type: Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrogen sulfide, produced water
Summary: On October 26, 2019, Pumper A responded to a pump oil level alarm at Aghorn's Foster D waterflood station. While attempting to isolate Pump #1 without performing Lockout/Tagout, the pump automatically energized, causing a broken plunger to release water containing hydrogen sulfide gas. Pumper A was fatally injured from H2S exposure. Subsequently, Pumper A's spouse gained access to the unlocked facility, was also exposed to H2S, and was fatally injured. The incident was attributed to multiple safety failures including nonuse of personal H2S detectors, lack of Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate ventilation, nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm systems, and deficient site security.
Recommendations:
Mandate use of personal H2S detection devices as integral part of PPE kit for all employees and visitors prior to entering facilities with H2S potential
Develop site-specific, formalized, comprehensive Lockout/Tagout program meeting 29 CFR 1910.147 requirements
Commission independent analysis of facility design regarding ventilation and mitigation systems for H2S release scenarios
Develop and demonstrate comprehensive safety management program addressing H2S hazards including risk identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring
Ensure H2S detection and alarm systems are properly maintained, configured, and include site-specific programs based on manufacturer specifications
Employ multiple layers of alerts (audible and visual) in H2S detection and alarm system design
Develop and implement formal, written, site-specific security program requiring employees to lock access gates upon entering and departing facility

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Nitrous oxide trailer truck explosion
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: nitrous oxide
Summary: On August 28, 2016, a nitrous oxide trailer truck exploded at the Airgas manufacturing facility in Cantonment, Florida, fatally injuring one Airgas employee and heavily damaging the facility. The most probable cause was that during initial loading of a trailer truck, a pump heated nitrous oxide above safe operating limits, likely starting a nitrous oxide decomposition reaction that propagated from the pump into the trailer truck, causing the explosion. The CSB determined that Airgas lacked an effective safety management system to identify, evaluate, and control process safety-related hazards. Following the incident, Airgas rapidly implemented comprehensive process safety management components for its nitrous oxide operations.
Recommendations:
Complete in-progress post-incident actions
Continue safety initiatives for nitrous oxide operations

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Organic Peroxide Decomposition and Fire
Incident Type: Chemical fire and vapor release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: organic peroxides, peróxido orgánico
Summary: On August 31, 2017, organic peroxides stored in refrigerated trailers at the Arkema Crosby facility decomposed and ignited following catastrophic flooding from Hurricane Harvey. The facility lost electrical power, backup generators, liquid nitrogen cooling systems, and refrigerated trailer cooling due to a common-mode failure caused by unprecedented flooding. Over 350,000 pounds of organic peroxide decomposed across three separate fires over 12 days. More than 200 residents evacuated and 21 emergency responders sought medical attention due to exposure to decomposition vapors.
Recommendations:
Facilities should perform analysis to determine susceptibility to extreme weather events
Evaluate flood maps to determine if facility is within 100 or 500-year floodplain
Evaluate seismic risk maps for potential earthquake risk
Consider risk of other extreme weather phenomena
Ensure critical protections and equipment are not susceptible to common-mode failures during high flood levels
Implement independent layers of protection available in case of high inundation levels
Develop systems to retain key summarized information from incidents documenting historical safety risks
Apply conservative risk management approach when evaluating impacts of extreme climate scenarios
Do not reopen evacuation zone roads until combustion is confirmed absent despite telemetry data

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Toxic Chlorine Gas Release
Incident Type: Chemical decomposition and fire resulting in toxic gas release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: trichloroisocyanuric acid (TCCA), TCCA-based formulations, chlorine gas, nitrogen trichloride
Summary: On August 27, 2020, Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to Bio-Lab's Lake Charles facility in Westlake, Louisiana. Extreme winds tore off portions of the roof of Plant 4, which stored approximately 70,000-100,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation. Rainwater from the hurricane contacted the stored TCCA-based formulation, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition that released toxic chlorine gas and caused a fire. The decomposition and fire destroyed Plant 4 and damaged additional structures. A massive plume of hazardous gases crossed the facility boundary and was visible over a large portion of the nearby community. Interstate 10 was closed for over 28 hours, and a shelter-in-place order was issued. No injuries were reported. Approximately $250 million was invested in redesigning and reconstructing the facility, which resumed operations around March 2023.
Recommendations:
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement processes and safeguards for protection, including constructing buildings to withstand hurricane winds and flooding, implementing safeguards to ensure hazardous chemicals are not compromised during extreme weather, and following CCPS guidance on natural hazards assessment and planning
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Develop and implement improved PHA action item management system with assignment, documentation, and tracking of all action items until resolution, and conduct periodic audits
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Perform PHAs on all buildings and units processing or storing TCCA, revalidate at least every five years, and include building design basis as process safety information
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Revise emergency response plan to require proper maintenance and function-testing of fire protection system, checking emergency equipment one month before hurricane season, and training personnel on emergency generators and equipment
Louisiana Governor and State Legislature: Require existing chemical manufacturing and storage facilities in hurricane-prone regions to evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement protective processes and safeguards
OSHA: Amend PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, broaden application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, and augment PHA element to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards
EPA: Revise Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals and process-specific conditions
EPA: Implement five open recommendations from 2022 GAO Report on chemical accident prevention related to climate change and natural hazards risks

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Naphtha Release and Fire
Incident Type: Flash Fire from Vapor Cloud Ignition
Operating Phase: production
Materials: naphtha, refinery fuel gas, crude oil
Summary: On September 20, 2022, at approximately 6:46 p.m., a vapor cloud ignited causing a flash fire at the BP-Husky Toledo Refinery in Oregon, Ohio. Two BP employees (brothers Ben and Max Morrissey) were fatally injured when they released approximately 23,000 pounds of flammable liquid naphtha from a pressurized Fuel Gas Mix Drum to the ground. The drum, which normally contained only vapor (fuel gas), had filled with liquid naphtha when an upstream Crude 1 Tower overflowed naphtha into a vapor bypass line. The incident resulted in approximately $597 million in property damage and is the largest fatal incident at a BP-operated petroleum refinery since the 2005 Texas City incident.
Recommendations:
Revise safeguards in process hazard analyses to establish effective preventive safeguards using engineered controls that do not rely solely on human intervention
Revise Abnormal Situation Management policy to incorporate CCPS guidance and include procedures for unplanned crude slate changes, continued operation with units shut down, and emergency pressure-relief valve opening
Develop and implement Stop Work Authority policy with clear procedures and regular training for employees to stop unsafe work during abnormal situations
Revise Toledo Alarm Philosophy to incorporate EEMUA guidance for alarm rate following upset and improve alarm performance to reduce alarm flood duration and peak rate
API should develop guidance on Fuel Gas Mix Drum design, sizing, instrumentation, and preventive safeguards
API should develop publication addressing prevention of pressure vessel overflow with guidance on instrumentation, process hazard analysis, safeguards, and training
ISA should revise ANSI/ISA 18.2-2016 to include performance targets for short-term alarm flood analysis

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Subsea Leak
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Rod Piston Seal Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydraulic fluid, natural gas
Summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.
Recommendations:
BSEE to consider developing policy to address potential SCSSV rod piston seal failures to prevent reoccurrence
BSEE to perform detailed investigation into rod piston seal failures affecting BP's subsea wells
Follow-up meeting scheduled between BSEE and BP engineering and leadership to investigate similarities in multiple rod piston seal failures over the last 3 years

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Pipeline Leak
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 8 June 2022 at 1310 hours, production personnel at MC 778 Thunderhorse South reported an external subsea leak at the P2 Riser Touchdown Connector - Pipeline End Termination (PLET) No. 5. The leak was discovered during a ROV survey initiated following a platform blackout on 5 June 2022. An estimated 12.8 to 128.1 gallons of crude oil was released. The root cause was determined to be connector failure on the PLET, potentially due to design flaw, improper installation, or manufacturing defects. The leak was temporarily sealed using a Connector Actuation Tool pending permanent replacement.
Recommendations:
Gulf of Mexico Regional Office Production Operation Support Section to review subsea leak detection system effectiveness
Conduct root cause analysis of connector leak once removed from service
Implement routine maintenance and pigging of Thunder Horse South Flowlines every 8-12 weeks to prevent buildup
Maintain P2 flowline out of service when not circulating or pigging until permanent replacement is completed

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Tank overfill and vapor cloud explosion
Incident Type: Loss of Containment - Overfill
Operating Phase: storage and distribution
Materials: unleaded gasoline, fuel oil, jet fuel, diesel fuel
Summary: On October 23, 2009, at approximately 12:23 a.m., a 5-million gallon aboveground storage tank (Tank 409) at the CAPECO facility in Bayamón, Puerto Rico overflowed during offloading of gasoline from the tanker ship Cape Bruny. Approximately 194,000 gallons of gasoline escaped, forming a large vapor cloud that encompassed 107 acres. The vapor cloud migrated through open dike drain valves to low-lying areas and the wastewater treatment area, where it ignited. The resulting explosion registered 2.9 on the Richter scale, damaged approximately 300 homes and businesses up to 1.25 miles away, caused over $5 million in damages to Fort Buchanan military facility, and resulted in fires that burned for nearly 60 hours, destroying or damaging 17 of 48 petroleum storage tanks. Environmental contamination included petroleum products leaking into soil, nearby wetlands, and navigable waterways.
Recommendations:
EPA should require risk assessments for bulk petroleum storage tank terminals considering proximity to communities and potential for multiple tank releases
OSHA should extend Process Safety Management (PSM) standard to tank terminal facilities
EPA should require high-level alarm systems and automatic overfill prevention systems under SPCC requirements
API should revise ANSI/API 2350 to require automatic overfill prevention systems for all facilities, not just remotely operated ones
NFPA should revise NFPA 30 to require independent or redundant level alarms and automatic overfill prevention systems
ICC should require independent audible or visual alarms to indicate rising liquid levels
Facilities should conduct comprehensive risk assessments considering site complexity, flammable liquid types, and community proximity
Emergency responders should conduct pre-incident planning with facilities for vapor cloud explosions and multiple tank fires
National incident database for tank terminal incidents should be established to assess frequency and develop tailored requirements

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Pipe rupture and fire
Incident Type: Loss of Containment with ignition
Operating Phase: production
Materials: light gas oil, crude oil, hydrogen sulfide, sulfur compounds
Summary: On August 6, 2012, a catastrophic rupture occurred in the 4-sidecut piping of the #4 Crude Unit at Chevron Richmond Refinery. The 52-inch component of the 8-inch line ruptured due to sulfidation corrosion, releasing approximately 10,800 barrels per day of light gas oil. The release vaporized into a large vapor cloud that engulfed 19 employees. Approximately two minutes after release, the vapor ignited, resulting in a large fire. One firefighter was caught in the fireball but escaped due to protective equipment. Six employees suffered minor injuries. Approximately 15,000 people from surrounding communities sought medical treatment. The incident resulted in a Community Warning System Level 3 alert and shelter-in-place advisory for Richmond, San Pablo, and North Richmond.
Recommendations:
Chevron to engage diverse team for documented damage mechanism hazard review as part of Process Hazard Analysis cycle
Include applicable industry best practices and inherently safer systems in hazard reviews
Report leading and lagging process safety indicators at all California Chevron refineries
Require documented use of inherently safer systems analysis and hierarchy of controls in establishing safeguards
California refineries to perform documented damage mechanism hazard reviews
Identify and require reporting of leading and lagging process safety indicators
Establish multi-agency process safety regulatory program for California oil refineries
EPA to jointly plan and conduct inspections with Cal/OSHA and state agencies
Enhance and restructure California's process safety management regulations for petroleum refineries
Implement compensation system to ensure regulator capability in process safety oversight

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Spill
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, oily water
Summary: On 19-OCT-2022, during facility startup after a three-day shutdown, the back pressure valve (BPV) for the NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater failed to operate as designed. The valve remained open, allowing 94 PSI of gas into the treater, causing the Pressure Safety Valve to relieve to the relief header and vent flare. The vent flare emitted a fine mist of oily water to the atmosphere. An estimated 0.774 gallons of oil entered Gulf of Mexico waters, with satellite imagery later detecting a sheen measuring 2.44 nautical miles in length and 2.67 nautical miles at the widest point. The incident was reported to the National Response Center.
Recommendations:
BSEE Lake Jackson District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations coordinate with the Office of Safety Management to follow up on Cox Operating, LLC operating procedures

Top Event: Fire at gas compressor engine starter
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 27 January 2022 at approximately 1625 hours, a fire occurred near the starter of a rental gas compressor on Main Pass 73-A production platform. The fire resulted from an improperly tightened JIC fitting on the engine's starter exhaust hose that backed off over time due to vibration, combined with a leaking Flow Safety Valve (FSV) that allowed natural gas to migrate to the hot exhaust area (>900 degrees). Four platform operators successfully extinguished the fire using handheld fire extinguishers within three minutes. No injuries or pollution occurred. Damage was limited to compressor wiring and FSV internal parts, estimated at $12,000.

N/A

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge via Vent Boom
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 25 February 2022 at 0530 hours, a pollution event occurred at West Delta 73 C production platform. An estimated 660 gallons (later revised to approximately 111 gallons) of unrecoverable oil was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the platform's vent boom. The incident resulted from a combination of a Water Skimmer float device failure (pinhole defect) and failure of the Level Safety High (LSH) device on the Vent Scrubber to operate as designed. Contributing factor was operator fatigue, with OP2 working 21 hours (0600 on 24 Feb to 0300 on 25 Feb) and becoming extremely fatigued before the incident occurred.
Recommendations:
BSEE New Orleans District has no recommendations for the Office of Incident Investigations at this time

Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Multiple incidents involving lack of remote isolation capability
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, naphtha, propylene, propane, butane, isobutylene, hydrofluoric acid, butadiene, natural gas
Summary: This CSB Safety Study examines six major incidents (1975-2019) where lack of effective remote isolation equipment contributed to escalation of loss-of-containment events, resulting in fatalities, serious injuries, environmental contamination, and massive property damage. The study concludes that regulatory requirements for remote isolation of process equipment are necessary to prevent future incidents.
Recommendations:
API: Develop new publication or revise existing publications requiring remote isolation devices for major process equipment and atmospheric storage tanks with mandatory 'shall' language
EPA: Update Risk Management Program rule to include evaluation of remote isolation devices for major process equipment
OSHA: Expand Process Safety Management standard to include evaluation of remote isolation devices in Process Hazard Analysis requirements
Industry: Assess remote isolation capabilities for major process equipment during hazard reviews and Process Hazard Analysis
Facilities: Install remotely operated emergency isolation valves configured to fail-closed for atmospheric storage tanks containing highly hazardous chemicals

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Anhydrous ammonia release to atmosphere
Incident Type: Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: anhydrous ammonia
Summary: On July 31, 2024, at approximately 8:20 p.m., an emergency pressure relief valve on Heat Exchanger 5 Surge Drum in Tank Farm 5 opened due to overpressure, discharging approximately 275 pounds of anhydrous ammonia to the atmosphere. The discharged ammonia contained a high level of liquid, causing it to rapidly drop to ground level near the employee parking lot and building. During evacuation, 33 workers were injured by ammonia inhalation, with 4 admitted to hospitals. The incident was caused by overpressure in the vessel, failure to discharge to a safe location, and insufficient emergency preparedness including lack of effective alarms and emergency shutdown procedures.
Recommendations:
IIAR to update ANSI/IIAR 2 to include guidance for preventing or mitigating liquid or two-phase atmospheric discharges from emergency pressure relief systems
IIAR to update ANSI/IIAR 2 to require assessment of whether emergency pressure relief devices discharge to a safe location
Cuisine Solutions to reduce likelihood or mitigate consequences of liquid or two-phase atmospheric discharges through engineering controls and emergency pressure control systems
Cuisine Solutions to implement electronic process data historian and management system
Cuisine Solutions to update Emergency Action Plan with separate indoor/outdoor release procedures, evacuation routes, windsock guidance, and annual drills
Cuisine Solutions to add ammonia-specific alarm system with documented response procedures

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Chemical leak in flying lead
Operating Phase: production
Materials: paraffin inhibitor, Nalco EC 6004A, naphthalene
Summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.
Recommendations:
Update operating procedures to ensure that umbilical lines are positively pressured
Provide operator training to ensure the line does not get bled to zero for any maintenance tasks
Add a check valve to the system topsides to ensure a positive pressure is maintained on the system
Add a low-pressure alarm on the chemical pump skid for any injection lines that are connected subsea with a hose

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Explosion with personnel injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, pyrophoric iron (FeS), flammable liquids
Summary: On 24 June 2022 at approximately 1147 hours, two Gulf Island Services (GIS) employees were performing construction work on Main Pass 260 Platform P, a right-of-way platform owned by Destin Pipeline Company located 55 miles offshore in 300 feet of water. While installing a skillet to isolate the flare scrubber and flare, workers unbolted a 10-inch flange and determined the skillet did not fit. While waiting for the correct size skillet, a handheld gas detector alarmed, pressure was heard escaping from the flange, and an explosion occurred with a large plume of smoke. Both workers reported burning sensations and smoke inhalation. Both were transported to an onshore medical facility and released the same day without restrictions. No pollution or facility damage occurred. BSEE investigation identified three plausible causes: pyrophoric iron (FeS) combustion when exposed to oxygen through the opened flange (most likely), galvanic cell corrosion from a chain hoist generating electric current, or hydrate release within the piping.
Recommendations:
BSEE recommends that the Office of Incident Investigations consider a safety alert warning operators about Pyrophoric Iron (FeS) and ways to mitigate risk of these reactions

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Combustible dust explosions and building collapse
Incident Type: Deflagration and secondary explosions
Operating Phase: production
Materials: corn dust, pregelatinized corn meal, corn products
Summary: On May 31, 2017, at approximately 11:00 p.m., multiple combustible dust explosions occurred at the Didion Milling dry corn milling facility in Cambria, Wisconsin. The incident fatally injured five employees and injured 14 others. During the day of the incident, Didion connected the discharge of two rotary gap mills to increase production. On the night of the incident, employees smelled smoke and investigated the source. A fire spread through process equipment via interconnected dust collection systems, resulting in explosions in dust collection equipment. Secondary explosions occurred throughout the facility, causing the collapse of multiple buildings including the Multipurpose Building, F Mill, and the Boiler House. The estimated property damage was $15,375,959 with no off-site consequences.
Recommendations:
Develop comprehensive combustible dust process safety management system including management of change, process safety information, audits and inspections, fugitive dust management, incident investigation, dust hazard analyses, engineering controls, PPE, and emergency preparedness
Modify pneumatic conveying and dust collector ductwork systems to ensure minimum transport velocity and implement periodic inspection and testing programs
Perform dust hazard analyses on all buildings and units processing combustible dust with revalidation every five years
Assess and implement engineering controls for structural design and venting of reconstructed facility
Incorporate paper-based process safety information into electronic records management system
Perform personal protective equipment hazard analyses and require flame-resistant garments for combustible dust operations
Update facility emergency response plan with signal/alarm system, emergency shutdown procedures, evacuation instructions, and response to fire scenarios
Assess and update pre-deflagration detection and suppression engineering controls
Develop and implement process safety leadership and culture program
Issue comprehensive OSHA combustible dust standard based on NFPA standards

Top Event: Fire from overpressurization and rupture of fuel gas filter vessel
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, hydrocarbon fluids, glycol
Summary: On 9 May 2015 at approximately 1728 hours, the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter vessel experienced catastrophic overpressurization during facility restart following a pressure upset. The vessel top was blown off, struck a nearby handrail creating sparks that ignited streaming fuel gas. The incident resulted in a fire that was extinguished by operators using portable fire extinguishers. Root causes included severe corrosion of vessel mounting tabs, failed pressure safety valves both upstream and downstream of the filter, unauthorized modifications to the filter vessels that removed differential gauges and altered level indicators, and a single-stage pressure reduction system that was inadequate for the operating conditions.
Recommendations:
Lake Jackson District reported no recommendations to regional office

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Oil Release
Incident Type: Pollution event with thermal burn injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hot pressurized oil
Summary: On June 23, 2014, at approximately 10:30 A.M., the Person in Charge (PIC) at Arena Offshore East Cameron 328 B facility was replacing a faulty pneumatic temperature controller (TC) on Heater Treater #2 (NBK-2770). The TC was installed directly into a thread-o-let within the liquid medium of the vessel instead of inside a thermo-well as recommended by the manufacturer. Prior to removal, the vessel was not depressurized and hot oil was not drained to a safe level. When the TC was removed, hot pressurized oil blew out of the one inch opening, striking and burning the PIC on his left bicep. Oil continued to flow across the deck, striking the line heater approximately 20 feet away. Operations personnel manually shut down the facility and installed a nipple and ball valve to isolate the leak. A total of 12 gallons of crude oil was discharged into offshore waters.
Recommendations:
Ensure temperature controllers are installed inside thermo-wells as recommended by manufacturer
Implement depressurization procedures prior to removing pressurized vessel components
Drain hot oil to safe levels below component removal points
Allow sufficient cooling time before maintenance activities
Enhance Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to identify hazards specific to non-standard equipment installations

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Equipment failure leading to fires and explosions
Operating Phase: production
Materials: methane, ethane, propane, natural gas liquids, hydrocarbons
Summary: On June 27, 2016, at approximately 11:22 p.m., a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX) on the A-Train process line failed due to thermal fatigue, resulting in a major loss of containment of flammable hydrocarbons. The released hydrocarbons ignited, initiating a series of fires and explosions that caused 13 different ruptures of piping and equipment over approximately 35 minutes. Two workers on the night shift were uninjured. The facility experienced extensive damage and was shut down for almost six months. Enterprise incurred $10.4 million in fire response expenses and a non-cash loss of $7.1 million in damaged assets. A total of 104,000 pounds of emissions were released, with most burned off by fires and flares.
Recommendations:
2016-02-I-MS-R1: American Petroleum Institute recommendation on BAHX thermal fatigue management
2016-02-I-MS-R2: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on BAHX integrity management
2016-02-I-MS-R3: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on industry data sharing
2016-02-I-MS-R4: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on BAHX operating guidance
2016-02-I-MS-R5: Jackson County Local Emergency Planning Committee recommendation on community notification and social media use

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Nitrogen bottle rupture and explosion
Incident Type: explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: nitrogen, jet fuel, diesel
Summary: On April 28, 2025, at approximately 10:30 a.m., a nitrogen bottle ruptured and exploded during refilling operations on the BW Pioneer FPSO in Walker Ridge Block 249. The bottle had been mistakenly assumed to be rated for 6,000 psi when it was only rated for 2,400 psi, and was recharged to more than double its maximum capacity. Additionally, an incorrect relief valve rated for 9,000 psi had been installed instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi valve. The explosion caused moderate to severe structural damage to the bottle rack, manifold, diesel fuel tank, and nearby equipment. A crane operator located approximately 18 feet away was struck by flying debris, sustaining minor bruises, a small neck lesion, and temporary hearing impairment. The root cause was over-pressurization due to equipment failure (incorrect relief valve) combined with human error (failure to identify correct pressure rating) and inadequate procedures and training.
Recommendations:
Implement formal written procedure for nitrogen bottle filling operations with detailed guidelines
Establish proper training program for personnel on cylinder markings, pressure ratings, and overpressure risks
Implement routine inspection and maintenance program for nitrogen bottles and relief devices
Ensure correct relief valves are installed matching cylinder pressure ratings
Update Job Safety Analysis to specify target fill pressure and identify overpressurization hazards
Properly identify and segregate nitrogen bottles by type (DOT vs UN-regulated) and pressure rating
Establish competency requirements for personnel interpreting cylinder markings

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Isobutane release and fire
Incident Type: Loss of Containment with Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: isobutane, sulfuric acid
Summary: On November 22, 2016, during removal of an inoperable gearbox on a plug valve in the sulfuric acid alkylation unit, an operator removed four vertical bolts that secured both the gearbox support bracket and the pressure-retaining top-cap. When the operator attempted to open the valve with a pipe wrench, the valve came apart and released approximately 2,000 pounds of pressurized isobutane, forming a flammable vapor cloud. The vapor reached an ignition source (an energized welding machine approximately 70 feet away) within 30 seconds, causing a fire that severely burned four workers (one ExxonMobil employee and three contractors) with third-degree burns.
Recommendations:
Evaluate human factors associated with equipment design and apply the hierarchy of controls to mitigate identified hazards
Establish detailed and accurate written procedures and provide training to ensure workers can perform all anticipated job tasks safely
Upgrade older model plug valves (pre-1984 design) to improved design that attaches gearbox support bracket to valve body flanges rather than pressure-retaining components
Develop written procedures detailing steps for safely removing various types of plug valve gearboxes
Provide formal training to operators on how to remove gearboxes from plug valves and associated hazards
Conduct human factors evaluation during Process Hazard Analysis to identify equipment design and reliability issues
Review existing process safety management practices and equipment design for potential improvements

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Well Control and Pollution Event
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydrocarbons, natural gas
Summary: On 10 January 2022, a well control and pollution event occurred at WD 80 D production platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident originated from the D-36 well where an alignment pin plug on the tubing head had been leaking gas since 11 January 2021. During a coiled tubing operation to set a plug and clean scale, the tool became stuck downhole and required mechanical extraction, which exacerbated tubing-to-casing communication. This caused the A Annulus pressure to rise from 50 psi to 2000 psi, resulting in excessive fluid returns to the wet oil tank. Approximately 107 gallons (2.5 barrels) of hydrocarbons were spilled. The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. Root causes included equipment failure (packer assembly design flaw, alignment pin corrosion due to inadequate coating maintenance), human performance error during coiled tubing operations, and organizational failures in communication and urgency to remediate.
Recommendations:
Operators should maintain proper coatings on wellhead equipment in corrosive saltwater environments
Well intervention plans should account for potential exacerbation of fluid returns and include adequate vessel and fluid handling capacity
Timely reporting of well anomalies to regulatory authorities is critical for prompt remediation
Scale and paraffin buildup should be managed proactively to enable safe well interventions
Management communication systems should ensure field observations are escalated and acted upon promptly

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - H2S Gas Release
Incident Type: H2S gas release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrogen sulfide (H2S), sour gas, crude oil and gas liquids
Summary: On 29-May-2016 at 1310 hrs, a hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas release of 400 ppm occurred on Main Pass 299-FP Platform during pigging operations on the flowline from MP 299-A to FP Platform. While the crew drained fluids from the pig trap, H2S sensors detected high concentrations of sour gas. The H2S alarm sounded and the platform was shut in. All personnel donned SCBAs and mustered. After an initial all-clear declaration, the H2S alarm sounded again. The response team located a gas leak in the 2-inch pipe outlet from the MBF-935 Buyback Gas Scrubber below the south side cellar deck. Investigation determined the leak was caused by extensive exterior corrosion that compromised the piping integrity. The root cause was identified as failure of the operator to perform routine corrosion assessment and maintenance on piping. The field was subsequently shut in indefinitely as continued operation was deemed uneconomical.
Recommendations:
BSEE New Orleans District made no recommendations to the Office of Incident Investigations

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Liquid Nitrogen Release
Incident Type: Cryogenic liquid release resulting in asphyxiation fatalities
Operating Phase: production
Materials: liquid nitrogen
Summary: On January 28, 2021, liquid nitrogen overflowed from an immersion freezer at Foundation Food Group's Plant 4 in Gainesville, Georgia. The overflow occurred while maintenance workers were troubleshooting operational issues with the freezer, which was designed and owned by Messer LLC and rented to FFG. Once released, the liquid nitrogen vaporized, expanded, and rapidly accumulated within a partially enclosed cold room lacking mechanical ventilation. Two maintenance workers died from asphyxiation due to vaporized nitrogen. The uncontrolled nitrogen leak continued undetected for 30-60 minutes. When discovered, at least 14 FFG employees responded by entering the cold room or attempting rescue, resulting in four additional fatalities and three employees plus one firefighter suffering severe asphyxiation symptoms. The incident was caused by failure of the liquid level control system due to deformation of the bubble-type level gauge component, combined with inadequate process safety management, emergency preparedness, atmospheric monitoring, and product stewardship practices.
Recommendations:
Gold Creek Foods (current facility owner) should proactively engage local emergency responders with information about hazardous substances, critical equipment locations, and emergency systems
Messer LLC should update product stewardship policy to include participation in customer PHAs, verify proper signage for oxygen-deficient atmospheres, and review emergency shutdown switch locations
Messer should create informational materials for customers on safety issues and effective safety management systems for inert gas asphyxiation hazards
OSHA should update Regional Emphasis Programs for poultry processing facilities (Regions 4 and 6) and food manufacturing (Region 5) to explicitly cover liquid nitrogen freezing processes
OSHA should promulgate a specific standard for cryogenic asphyxiant agents addressing equipment design, atmospheric monitoring, emergency shutdown systems, employee training, emergency action plans, and process safety management elements
OSHA should develop guidance document for process safety management practices applicable to compressed gases and cryogenic asphyxiant agents
Compressed Gas Association (CGA) should develop comprehensive standard for safe storage, handling, and use of liquid nitrogen in fixed facilities
CGA should update P-76 standard on oxygen-deficient atmospheres to require atmospheric monitoring systems, distinct alarm indications, remote-operated emergency isolation valves (ROEIV), and guidance on emergency stop device locations
National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) should update NFPA 55 to require atmospheric monitoring with cryogenic asphyxiant agents and include guidance on safe location of manual isolation valves and emergency activation devices
International Code Council (ICC) should update International Fire Code to require atmospheric monitoring with cryogenic asphyxiant agents and include guidance on safe location of isolation valves and emergency devices

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment from Aboveground Storage Tank
Incident Type: Chemical spill to environment and drinking water contamination
Operating Phase: storage and distribution
Materials: Crude MCHM (methylcyclohexanemethanol), PPH, stripped (polyglycol ethers), Shurflot 944
Summary: On January 9, 2014, approximately 11,000 gallons of a mixture containing Crude MCHM and PPH, stripped leaked from tank 396 at Freedom Industries facility in Charleston, West Virginia. The leak resulted from pitting corrosion that created two holes (0.75 and 0.4 inches diameter) in the tank floor. The chemical mixture escaped through failed secondary containment and an underground culvert, flowing into the Elk River approximately 1.5 miles upstream of the West Virginia American Water treatment facility intake. The contamination resulted in a Do Not Use order affecting approximately 300,000 residents across nine counties. Over 300 people sought medical treatment for symptoms including nausea, rashes, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. The incident exposed significant gaps in tank inspection, secondary containment maintenance, leak detection systems, source water protection, and public health communication.
Recommendations:
AST owners and operators should establish regular inspection programs and routinely monitor tanks and secondary containment to verify tank integrity
AST owners and operators should coordinate with nearby water utilities and emergency response organizations to ensure chemical information is immediately available
AST owners and operators should ensure spill prevention and protection plans are updated and implemented
States should take immediate action to protect source waters through increased inspections and enforcement at chemical storage facilities near water sources
States should establish Source Water Assessment Programs that mandate source water protection planning by water utilities
Water utilities should engage with Local Emergency Planning Committees to obtain Tier II information about stored chemicals
Water utilities should assess treatment system capabilities to remove potential hazardous chemicals
Public health agencies should establish communication frameworks to ensure information is coordinated through one entity
American Water Works Association should disseminate lessons learned from chemical contamination incidents
American Water should establish requirements for subsidiary surface water treatment plants to develop source water protection plans

Top Event: Explosion and Fire at Pig Launcher
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, sales gas
Summary: On November 8, 2017 at 1:18 a.m., an explosion occurred at the 30-inch sales gas pipeline pig launcher on the Enchilada platform (GB Block 128) operated by Shell Offshore Inc. Two operators were preparing to launch a pig when the pig launcher closure door failed explosively, propelling the door and other items overboard. A fire ensued, fed by natural gas from the pipeline. One operator sustained concussion, back injuries, and second-degree burns; the other suffered minor injuries. All 46 platform personnel were safely evacuated. The root cause was incomplete closure of the clamp rings due to procedural failures in securing the closure door, combined with inadequate hazard analysis, improper management of change, and insufficient supervision.
Recommendations:
GOMR Office of Safety Management should issue a Safety Alert/Bulletin emphasizing adherence to established procedures, including lockout/tagout
GOMR Office of Safety Management should perform a SEMS program evaluation of GB-128 'A' facility in areas of Hazard Analysis, Operating Procedures, Safe Work Practices, and Mechanical Integrity

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Base Mud Spill
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic base mud (SBM), 16.9 ppg drilling fluid
Summary: At approximately 23:00 hours on 23 May 2014, the Diamond Ocean Onyx semi-submersible drilling rig reported a loss of synthetic base mud (SBM) during abandonment operations on Well #1. Initial report of 107 bbl was revised to 61 bbl after strapping calculations. The spill occurred when mud line valves on the Schlumberger Cement Unit were left in the open position after cleaning operations and were not checked prior to the cement job. When the Cementer began pumping cement from Batch Mixing Tank #2, the tank overflowed, causing SBM to spill into secondary containment areas and then into drains leading to the overboard discharge line, which also had open valves. The Toolpusher closed the overboard drain line valves at 23:30 hours, preventing further discharge. No injuries or property damage occurred.

The Toolpusher closed the overboard drain line valves at 23:30 hours, preventing further discharge. No injuries or property damage occurred.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea BOP Seal Failure
Incident Type: Leak / Pollution
Operating Phase: exploration
Materials: synthetic oil base mud (SOBM), drilling fluid
Summary: On 7 June 2017, the Noble Don Taylor drillship under contract to Shell reported an unauthorized discharge of approximately 94.2 barrels of 15.1 ppg synthetic oil base mud during exploration operations on Well DC005 (Cardamom) in Garden Banks Block 427, Gulf of Mexico. The spill was caused by failure of the upper variable bore ram (VBR) aft bonnet door cartridge seal on the subsea blowout preventer. Contributing factors included buildup of debris in the upper VBR body, failure to conduct proper hole cleaning and flushing of the BOP as recommended in NOV Product Information Bulletin released 11 August 2015, and inadequate communication of known risk of loss of seal integrity.
Recommendations:
Implement proper hole cleaning and flushing procedures for subsea BOP prior to operation
Improve communication of known risks identified in equipment manufacturer bulletins
Install cartridge guard to protect cartridge and seal from debris buildup and compression

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Unauthorized discharge of synthetic oil base mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic oil base mud (SOBM), 15.3 ppg drilling fluid
Summary: On 23 March 2015 at 0505 hours, the Rowan Reliance drill ship under contract to Cobalt International Energy reported a discharge of approximately 2200 barrels of 15.3 ppg synthetic oil base mud (SOBM) during well operations on Well 002 (North Platte #2) in Garden Banks Block 958 at 4846 ft water depth. The primary cause was the failure to install a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in the flange connection between the flex joint termination spool and the first riser joint (Joint #64). Contributing factors included inadequate procedures, insufficient crew handover, failure to recognize the SOBM buildup during ROV coverage, command structure issues, and under-torqued bolts (1250 psi instead of required 1600 psi). Of the 2200 bbl lost, 1557 bbl entered open water and 643 bbl were lost in open hole. Property damage totaled $151,353.
Recommendations:
BSEE Lafayette District recommends a Safety and Environmental Management Systems (SEMS) audit be conducted concurrently during Cobalt internal audit scheduled for November 2015

Top Event: Gas Release
Incident Type: Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: well abandonment
Materials: natural gas, hydrocarbon, calcium bromide brine (CaBr2), nitrogen
Summary: On 15 November 2024 at 0837 hours, a gas release occurred in the shaker house aboard the Valaris DS-16 drillship during well abandonment operations. While displacing the kill line and landing string back to calcium bromide brine after nitrogen displacement, hydrocarbons were released into the shaker house atmosphere. The incident was triggered by a compromised liquid leg in the mud gas separator, allowing hydrocarbon gas to bypass the separator and enter the shaker house. A hydrocarbon alarm activated, prompting evacuation and crew muster. Gas levels returned to zero and operations resumed without further issues.
Recommendations:
Safety meeting with all crews on importance of circulating fluids/gases from well and impact on mud gas separator liquid leg

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - LP Pipe Rupture
Incident Type: Pipe Rupture / Hydrocarbon Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, scale inhibitor, demulsifier
Summary: On 16-OCT-2015 at 2015 hours, an 18-inch LP pipe ruptured on the Front Runner spar at GC 338A, releasing approximately 25 barrels of oil and undetermined amount of gas. The rupture occurred at the chemical injection point where the line penetrated the production deck. Root cause was severe internal corrosion (metal loss up to 50.4%) caused by acidic chemical injection without proper distribution via quills. Contributing factors included failure to use quills for chemical distribution and inadequate NDT testing that did not cover injection point areas. One employee was injured. Total estimated damage: $1.5 million.
Recommendations:
Implement proper chemical injection distribution using quills at all injection points
Establish comprehensive NDT testing protocol that includes chemical injection point areas
Conduct piping inspections throughout structure at all chemical injection locations
Monitor for ongoing corrosion issues and implement preventive maintenance program

Top Event: Unauthorized discharge of Zinc Bromide due to Emergency Disconnect Sequence activation
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2), drilling fluid
Summary: On January 15, 2022, at approximately 16:10 hours, the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced a Dynamic Position (DP) system failure due to misalignment of Thruster-2 (180 degrees out of alignment) during completion operations at Green Canyon Block 389. High winds and sudden wind direction changes caused the misaligned thruster to push the drillship in the same direction as the wind, compromising DP position into red alert status. The Emergency Disconnect Sequence was initiated, unlatching the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) from the Blow Out Preventers (BOP). This resulted in an unauthorized discharge of approximately 420-558 barrels of 15.1 PPG Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2) into the Gulf of Mexico. The incident was contributed to by the operator's failure to complete the submitted Well Suspension Procedure in a safe and timely manner despite knowing inclement weather was approaching.
Recommendations:
Operator to send letter of explanation to Houma District Office addressing INC G-110 and detailing prevention measures for future incidents

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in Pressurized Welding Enclosure
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: welding materials, Panther felt, electrical wiring
Summary: On June 27, 2022, at approximately 1755 hours, a fire occurred inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Northeast side of Green Canyon Block 645 Platform A (Holstein). The fire was caused by improperly wrapped Panther felt on Zone 4 deluge wiring, which allowed slag from hot work to smolder inside the wrapping. The fire was discovered when the firewater pump activated and deluge Zone 4 was triggered by the burning wires that controlled the deluge system. Contributing factors included only one Firewatch being present during the 30-minute cool-down period instead of the required two, and fire and smoke detection systems being disabled when the hot work control panel was turned off.
Recommendations:
Add a second Firewatch to PWE hot work operations to remain on station for the full 30 minutes after hot work is complete
Add the ability of the control room to monitor the status of at least one fire and one gas detection at all times
Separate power supply for fire and smoke detection so that monitoring can continue even after hot work has concluded

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Load - Drill Pipe Fall
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: On February 14, 2021 at 0330 hours, a stand of 5.5-inch drill pipe fell approximately 4 feet from the Pipe Racking System (PRS) gripper to the rotary table during drilling operations on the Holstein Spar Rig. The PRS indicator panel displayed that the gripper was initiated to the closed position, but the pipe was not actually properly latched. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by lack of a positive latch indicator on the PRS panel and failure of the rig crew to visually verify that the drill pipe was properly gripped, despite JSA recommendation to do so.
Recommendations:
Update JSA and Standard Operation Procedure to assign a required flagger while operating the PRS to ensure pipe is properly gripped prior to racking back drill pipe
Evaluate availability of upgrading the PRS with a positive latch indicator

Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: At approximately 2040 hours on 06-SEP-2018, a high gas alarm on the main deck gas compression skid tripped on the Stampede floating production platform (OCS-G 26313, Green Canyon 468-A). Investigation revealed the gas release was caused by four loose fastening bolts on the sight glass of the Flash Gas Compressor 2-A Gas Filter Coalescer (MAJ-1950A). The loose bolts allowed the gasket to cut out under normal operating pressure (~300 psi), resulting in gas release and platform emergency shutdown. No injuries were reported.
Recommendations:
Conduct thorough inspection of all similar type of sight glasses before resuming production

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: High pressure gas release
Incident Type: Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: sales gas, natural gas
Summary: On May 8, 2018 at approximately 2115 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred on sales gas meter #3 at the Atlantis platform in Green Canyon Block 787-A. The release was discovered by third party personnel and lasted approximately 30 minutes. The root cause was the installation of incorrect Teflon seals instead of required Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) during a transmitter replacement 18 days prior. The seal failed at approximately 2100 psi. This was the fourth gas release incident on BP facilities since 2008 and the third on Atlantis, all due to failed Teflon seals. Contributing factors included lack of knowledge by the technician, poor oversight, inadequate training, bypassed gas detectors, insufficient detector placement, and deficient emergency response procedures.
Recommendations:
Implement process for managing third party equipment owners doing work on BP facilities
Ensure third party technicians receive sufficient training prior to performing work on facilities
Verify that third party technicians follow OEM procedures during equipment installation
Install additional gas detection sensors as recommended in 2011 and 2015 F&G assessments
Improve gas detection system placement and coverage
Enhance emergency response procedures and personnel training
Improve safety culture and communication between BP leadership and technicians

Top Event: Flash fire in waste oil incinerator tank
Incident Type: fire
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: waste oil
Summary: On December 10, 2016, at 05:40 a.m., a flash fire occurred inside the waste oil incinerator tank on the Pacific Drilling Sharav drillship operating for Chevron in Green Canyon block 807. The fire was caused by failure of the waste oil level switch to shut down power to the internal heating element when the waste oil level became too low. The exposed heating element ignited fumes inside the tank, causing a flash fire that distorted and ballooned the tank. One isolation valve was damaged and approximately 1 gallon of waste oil was released to secondary containment. No injuries or environmental threat occurred.
Recommendations:
Raise the suction line inside the tank above the heating element to eliminate the possibility of future flash fires

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Leak from Tubing Connection
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 16 July 2019 at 0700 hours, approximately 4.8 barrels of oil leaked from a 3/4" broken tubing connection (Swagelok fitting) on the gas lift bleed ring of the casing valve at Well R-22 on the unmanned GI 22-R platform. The leak was discovered when an operator on nearby GI 22-L observed a sheen north of the platform. The platform had been shut-in since 28 June 2019 for maintenance, but pressure remained on the gas lift line and well casing. High seas from Hurricane Barry (14-16 ft with occasional 21 ft seas) caused well movement that sheared the tubing. Contributing factors included inadequate tubing support, improperly functioning check valves in gas lift mandrels, incorrect installation of a Flow Safety Valve upstream rather than downstream of the tubing connection, and cleanup procedures that exacerbated pollution to a wildlife refuge.
Recommendations:
BSEE NOD recommends that OSM and OII develop a safety alert to address tubing supports. Tubing supports should be installed in such a way as to prevent stress and sheering.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, produced water
Summary: On January 26, 2018, at the GI 43 AP Platform in the Gulf of Mexico, a loss of containment of hydrocarbons and gas releases occurred. Control Room Operators experienced abnormal operating conditions due to sand and sludge buildup and chemical flocking affecting level sensors. Safety devices were placed in bypass to prevent equipment shutdown while operators flushed level bridles. A hole was discovered in the Gas Vent Header Piping at 1:18 am leaking hydrocarbons. The leak was temporarily sealed with duct tape and absorbent pad. Due to communication failures and personnel falling asleep, the platform continued flowing until 6:45 am when a sheen was observed and a field-wide shut-in was initiated. The incident resulted in a 10-mile sheen and discharge from 5 locations on the platform. Root causes included sludge buildup, collapsed internal packing, failed sump pumps, safety device bypass, and failure to use Stop Work Authority.
Recommendations:
Fieldwood submitted Corrective Action Plan #19115
Fieldwood announced new safety culture initiative 'Safe and Sound' emphasizing Stop Work Authority

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pipeline Leak
Incident Type: Pollution Event
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydrocarbons
Summary: On 25 August 2018 at 0730 hours, a 3/8-inch hole developed in a 12-inch departing pipeline (KAH-101, segment #8217) at Fieldwood Energy's Grand Isle 76-A platform, discharging approximately 8.35 barrels (352.27 gallons) of hydrocarbons into the Gulf of Mexico. The Pressure Safety Low sensor detected the leak and shut in the platform. Investigation revealed the pipeline had been converted from gas sales/condensate service to bulk oil service without BSEE approval. Contributing factors included improper Pressure Safety High (PSH) set point at 1200 psi (too close to MAOP of 1203 psi) and improper Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) set point at 1323 psi, both failing to provide adequate overpressure protection. The pipeline pumps' Maximum Discharge Pressure of 1800 psi exceeded the pipeline's MAOP, creating overpressure risk.
Recommendations:
BSEE New Orleans District Office referred findings to BSEE regional pipeline division for further review of Fieldwood Energy's pipeline modifications
BSEE Accident Investigator referred discrepancies on Safety Function Diagram (SFD) and Safety Analysis Function Evaluation (SAFE) chart to Production Operations Section Chief for operator correction

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Gas Release
Incident Type: Struck by Object / Pressure Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 25 April 2025 at approximately 1330 hours, a Louisiana Safety Systems, Inc. Valve Technician was performing maintenance on a control valve located on a 6-inch gas line at GI 47 AP platform. While disassembling the valve, a sudden pressurized gas release occurred, causing the valve bonnet flange assembly (approximately 50 pounds) to strike the technician in the left chest and abdomen area. The technician was knocked backwards onto an elevated scaffolding platform and possibly lost consciousness. The technician sustained a laceration behind the ear and an anterior rib fracture of the 8th left rib. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate communication between the operator and contractor, failure to obtain required work permits and PIC approval, failure to verify zero pressure on the gas line through proper energy isolation procedures, use of unapproved scaffolding without fall protection, and non-adherence to manufacturer recommendations for valve disassembly.
Recommendations:
Management to meet with GI 47 AP leadership to review incident learnings and contributing factors
Site leadership must ensure tasks involving pressurized systems are authorized by PIC with appropriate documentation prior to work beginning
Third-party service personnel working on pressurized systems must be accompanied by operations group member to verify double-block and bleed and line bled-down verification
Scaffolding contractors to post red caution tape around unapproved scaffolding as additional visual indicator
Task JSA development to include collective discussion of all hazards and proper mitigation through clear communication
Task JSAs to include mitigation of maintaining four nuts on bolts to prevent flange dislodgement from unforeseen pressure releases
Management to develop and distribute Safety Alert describing case findings, learnings, and prevention actions
BSEE to consider issuing a Safety Alert regarding the incident

Top Event: Backfire/Explosion
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: fuel gas, crude oil
Summary: On 16 March 2015 at approximately 14:48 hours, a backfire/explosion occurred at Chevron USA's High Island A582 'C' facility during platform startup after monthly Emergency Shut Down testing. Production Operators were bringing the platform back on line when the purge timer on Ambitrol Heater EAL-1510 was observed not counting down. Investigation revealed an inadequate purge of air within the Ambitrol Heater Cabin before ignition, allowing an explosive fuel mixture to accumulate. The air/fuel ratio valve linkage was initially disconnected, and the Programmable Logic Controller allowed excess fuel introduction during ignition. The unit backfired and dislodged the heater cover. No personnel injury, environmental incident, or fire resulted.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Consciousness in Confined Space
Incident Type: Confined Space Entry - Near Fatality
Operating Phase: production
Materials: heater treater fluids, sludge, hydrogen sulfide, hydrocarbons
Summary: On September 4, 2023, at approximately 09:15 hours, an injured person (IP) entered the NBK-1070 Heater Treater vessel on platform HIA 379 B without a Supplied Air Respirator (SAR), wearing only an Air Purifying Respirator (APR). The IP was overcome by toxic fumes and passed out face down in the vessel fluids and sludge. A pump operator noticed the IP missing and alerted the cleaning crew supervisor. The supervisor entered without a respirator, was also overcome, and had to exit. A construction supervisor then entered without respiratory protection, extracted the IP, and CPR was administered for approximately 20 minutes before the IP began breathing. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated in intensive care for three days. The incident resulted from failure to follow the confined space entry plan, including no tri-gas testing, no hole watch, improper PPE, and no rescue equipment setup.
Recommendations:
Ensure all Subcontractors adhere to work plans
Ensure all Company and Subcontracted personnel are properly trained before task/work is started
Ensure accurate incident reporting is made, to include follow up reports

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pipeline Discharge
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, wet gas
Summary: On May 26, 2022, BSEE inspectors observed a sheen approximately 30 yards by 200 yards in Gulf of Mexico waters originating from Cox Operating's High Island A 582 C facility. Investigation determined hydrocarbons were discharging from the incoming KAQ 2700 high pressure 8-inch wet gas pipeline segment number 13380 at the plus 10 deck level. The source was identified as a one-quarter inch diameter hole in the pipe due to corrosion. An estimated total of 129.145 gallons of oil was discharged during the bleed down process. The pipeline was repaired with a clamp and returned to service on June 17, 2022.
Recommendations:
Improve mechanical integrity program for all equipment

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Hydrogen Fluoride Release
Incident Type: Multiple incidents: gasket failure with fatality (Oct 2021), reboiler explosion (Jan 2023), HF release with serious injury (Jun 2024)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrogen fluoride (anhydrous HF), HFC-245fa, chlorine
Summary: Three separate hydrogen fluoride incidents occurred at Honeywell Geismar's HFC-245fa unit between October 2021 and June 2024. The first incident (October 21, 2021) involved catastrophic failure of a spiral wound gasket in a flanged piping connection, releasing 39 pounds of anhydrous HF and fatally injuring Field Operator 2 who was exposed to the toxic vapor. The second incident (January 23, 2023) involved catastrophic failure of a reboiler that had thinned due to HF corrosion, releasing over 800 pounds of anhydrous HF and over 1,600 pounds of chlorine gas; no personnel were injured but a complex-wide shelter-in-place was initiated. The third incident (June 7, 2024) involved partial removal of a blind flange from a valve containing pressurized HF during maintenance, releasing less than one pound of HF and seriously injuring a contract worker. All three incidents were preventable through effective implementation of existing safety management systems.
Recommendations:
Perform comprehensive third-party audit of Geismar facility's process safety and allied management systems evaluating compliance with federal standards and internal management systems (2023-02-I-LA-R1)
Require periodic reporting updates from Geismar site regarding closure of audit findings (2023-02-I-LA-R2)
Perform Safer Technologies and Alternatives Analysis (STAA) for HFC-245fa unit (2023-02-I-LA-R3)
EPA to initiate prioritization of hydrogen fluoride as High-Priority Substance under TSCA and conduct risk evaluation (2023-02-I-LA-R4)
OSHA to amend PSM standard to require MOC review for organizational changes impacting process safety (reiterated 2005-04-I-TX-R9)

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion and Fire
Incident Type: Pressure Cooker Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: zinc, cadmium, lead, iron, zinc oxide, silicon carbide
Summary: On 22 July 2010 at 14:20-16:19, a fatal explosion and fire occurred on Column B, a cadmium distillation column at the Horsehead zinc smelter in Monaca, Pennsylvania. The incident resulted from a partial blockage of the column sump that allowed liquid zinc to back-fill up the column, creating a pressure cooker effect. As the column filled with liquid zinc, internal pressure increased, raising the boiling point of zinc from 907°C to 980°C and creating superheated conditions. The column operated outside design parameters with pressure surges as vapour attempted to escape. Eventually, tray walls failed under the increased internal pressure, releasing a surge of superheated zinc vapour and liquid that ignited explosively, destroying the combustion chamber wall and creating a major conflagration in the workplace. The root causes included sump design with restricted clearance (25.625 inches or 65mm), poorly executed column commissioning that allowed formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion, high and increasing zinc throughput, inadequate sump temperatures during heat-up, and human factors including normalisation of hazardous conditions due to history of column blockages.
Recommendations:
Increase sump underflow clearance from 1 brick (65mm) to 2 bricks (130-180mm) minimum to allow passage of zinc, dross and debris
Implement best practice standards of management and training
Improve operator awareness and plant information systems
Take all possible steps to prevent air ingress to the column at feed point, condenser and sump
Improve dross separation at feedbox
Implement constant checking that column sump is running normally to prevent accumulation of liquid zinc
Avoid too low a run-off operating regime to prevent formation and accumulation of hard zinc compounds
Ensure adequate sump temperatures during column heat-up (target 650°C minimum)
Implement procedures to detect sump blockages early through nostril box inspections for dark trays
Change feed arrangement from top-down to bottom-up needle valve configuration to reduce dross and air entrainment
Maintain steady feed and firing conditions to minimize internal pressure disturbances
Implement monitoring of liquation pot temperatures to prevent excessive iron recycling (target maximum 450°C)
Improve commissioning procedures to prevent formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion
Establish clear emergency procedures that address sump blockage risks on all column types, not just zinc oxide columns

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment and Fire
Incident Type: Storage Tank Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: butane-enriched naphtha, naphtha, butane, hydrocarbon and petrochemical products
Summary: On March 17, 2019, a mechanical seal failure on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump resulted in release of approximately 177+ barrels of butane-enriched naphtha product over approximately 30 minutes. The released flammable vapors accumulated undetected in the absence of gas detection systems and ignited at 10:00:46 a.m. The resulting fire spread to 14 adjacent tanks in the First & Second 80's tank farm due to tank spacing, design, and lack of remotely operated isolation valves. The fire burned for three days, destroying 15 tanks and their contents. A containment wall breach released an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of hydrocarbon products, foam, and contaminated water into Tucker Bayou, Buffalo Bayou, and the Houston Ship Channel. The incident caused no fatalities or injuries but resulted in significant environmental contamination, community disruption, and over $150 million in property damage.
Recommendations:
Develop and implement a process safety management system for all atmospheric storage tanks and associated equipment in highly hazardous chemical service at ITC Deer Park terminal
Develop and implement a condition monitoring program for all pumps in highly hazardous chemical service with vibration alarms per ANSI/HI 9.6.9-2018
Install flammable gas detection systems with alarm functions in product storage and transfer areas where flammable substance releases could occur
Install remotely operated emergency isolation valves configured to Fail-Closed for all atmospheric storage tanks containing highly hazardous chemicals or liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating
Conduct evaluation of tank farm design against API STD 2610 and NFPA 30 standards, addressing tank spacing, subdivisions, process equipment placement, and drainage systems
Update API STD 2610 to include flammable gas detection systems in leak detection section
Eliminate the atmospheric storage tank exemption from OSHA PSM standard
Modify EPA RMP rule to expand coverage to include all flammable liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Casing Shoe Failure
Incident Type: Pollution - Unauthorized discharge of drilling mud
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Based Drilling Mud (SBM), Nitrogen foamed cement, Seawater
Summary: On 1 April 2015, the SeaDrill West Vela drill ship under contract to BP reported a dark area identified by ROV approximately 400-500 feet from the wellhead at Well 001 (Tiber #3) in Keathley Canyon Block 147. Samples from the seafloor showed gelatinous material resembling filter cake with 2% oil content, consistent with Synthetic Based Drilling Mud used during the 16.5 inch hole section drilling. The incident resulted from a 22 inch casing shoe failure at 8630 ft md/tvd set in a salt formation. From 18-22 January 2015, approximately 500 barrels of 14.4 ppg synthetic oil base mud were discharged into offshore waters during 14 inch intermediate casing running and cementing operations. Total mud losses during casing operations reached approximately 4802 barrels.
Recommendations:
Management should create a Condition of Approval (COA) by the District Operations Support (DOS) Section to place on all drilling permits requiring operators to contact the district before continuing operations when extreme losses are experienced during drilling and casing running operations in any hole section
