incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Equipment rupture and flammable vapor cloud explosion
Incident Type: Explosion and fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: isobutylene, sulfurized isobutylene
Summary: On April 2, 2019, at 10:51 a.m., a 3-inch cast iron y-strainer in the isobutylene supply piping ruptured due to brittle overload fracture driven by internal pressure from liquid thermal expansion. The rupture released more than 10,000 pounds of flammable isobutylene, creating a vapor cloud. KMCO operators manually closed valves and activated firewater systems to mitigate the release. However, the isobutylene vapor cloud ignited while two operators were still in or near the vapor cloud, fatally injuring one operator (James Mangum) and seriously injuring two others. At least 28 workers total were injured. The explosion and subsequent fires caused substantial facility damage. KMCO filed for bankruptcy in May 2020.
Recommendations:
Establish clear emergency response policies distinguishing which incidents operators should handle versus those requiring emergency response team response
Provide remote isolation equipment (remotely operated valves) to allow workers to stop chemical releases from safe locations such as blast-resistant control rooms
Implement comprehensive hazard evaluation programs that identify brittle materials in hazardous applications and potential high-pressure conditions
Install pressure-relief devices on piping sections subject to potential high-pressure conditions from thermal expansion
Ensure emergency response procedures and training prioritize life safety and do not rely on operators putting themselves in danger to stop releases
Activate facility alarm systems during chemical emergencies to ensure all workers receive notification

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fired heater tube rupture and fire
Incident Type: Loss of Containment with Fire
Operating Phase: startup
Materials: renewable diesel, hydrogen, fuel gas
Summary: On November 19, 2023, at 12:21 a.m., a metal tube ruptured within a fired heater during initial startup of a renewable diesel hydroprocessing unit at Marathon Martinez Renewables facility in Martinez, California. The rupture released over 200,000 pounds of renewable diesel and approximately 2,200 pounds of hydrogen, resulting in a fire that seriously injured one field operator with third-degree burns over 80 percent of his body. The incident was caused by overheating of fired heater tubes due to: (1) a misaligned (open) valve that diverted significant process flow away from the heater, and (2) two of four burners operating with cover plates blocking air inlets, leading to afterburning in the convection section. The field operator was positioned next to the heater during troubleshooting because adequate safe operating limits and alarms were not in place to alert personnel of unsafe conditions, and personnel chose to troubleshoot rather than remotely shut down the heater.
Recommendations:
Implement engineering safeguards to detect and prevent afterburning in the fired heater (combustibles measurements, flame detectors, thermocouples)
Implement tube metal temperature alarming consistent with corporate guidance with predetermined response actions including remote shutdown and personnel removal
Improve Walk the Line performance through operator field walkdowns, shift communications, and training on valve alignment
Complete comprehensive gap assessment of Martinez facility against Marathon corporate policies
Update corporate Process Heater Not-to-Exceed Limits and Alarms standard with tube metal temperature alarming and predetermined response actions
Update corporate Heater Application Standard with requirements for protecting fired heaters from low process flow and afterburning detection
Conduct Operations Excellence full assessment on Martinez facility
Revise API RP 556 with requirements for high tube metal temperature response, low process flow protection, and afterburning detection

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: An explosion occurred on 14 February 2019 at 0115 on the Mississippi Canyon 109 A (Amberjack) Platform. The explosion originated from a 12 Volt battery located inside a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) Panel used for gas sales measurement and communication. A rechargeable battery had been overcharged due to improper replacement and installation in a non-vented enclosure, causing hydrogen gas accumulation and ignition of electrical components. The panel door was blown off and debris landed 45 feet away. No injuries or pollution occurred. The root cause was flawed equipment design and installation of non-rated electrical components in a hazardous area, compounded by inadequate personnel training on battery hydrogen venting and lack of hazard analysis during design changes.
Recommendations:
The New Orleans District recommends that the Office of Incident Investigations considers drafting a Safety Alert regarding electrical components and hazardous areas

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Overboard Discharge of Drilling Fluid
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM)
Summary: At approximately 0055 hrs on 1 April 2019, LLOG Exploration Offshore LLC experienced a spill of 61 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) on the Seadrill West Neptune Drillship during drilling operations at Mississippi Canyon Block 199. The spill resulted from a misalignment of trip tank valve positions. While the Derrickman was transferring mud between trip tanks per driller request, the master dump valve for the Trip Tanks was found to be locked in the open position, allowing 61 bbls of SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line into the Gulf of Mexico. The pit cleaning crew had possibly locked open the master dump valve during cleaning operations and never returned it to its normally closed and locked position. Total pollution was 37.8 bbls (61 bbls at 62% synthetic make-up).

N/A

Top Event: Fire in turbine generator enclosure
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, natural gas
Summary: On 30 November 2021 at 1917 hours, a fire occurred in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure at the Appomattox semi-submersible production platform. Flame detectors alerted the control room, and a deck operator confirmed smoke and sparks from the generator housing. The generator ESD was activated, platform ESD initiated, and full muster sounded. Fire teams extinguished the fire with water. Investigation determined that vibration caused loss of fastener preload in the exhaust hatch bolts, allowing hot exhaust to escape and ignite Visqueen (plastic tarp) left by painters, which then melted cables. No injuries, explosions, or environmental harm occurred.

Summary: On 15 July 2020 at 1411 hours, the Appomattox platform (MC 437 A) experienced an unplanned gas release while attempting to start up Bulk Gas Compressor #1. Top Event: Unauthorized discharge of synthetic oil based mud overboard
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM), salt water, seawater
Summary: On 13 February 2015 at 1115 hours, a 260 barrel discharge of 15 ppg Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) occurred at MC 762 on the Stena Forth. The Derrickman intended to transfer salt water from active pit #2 to active pit #4 but was unaware that SOBM from equalized active pits #1 and #3 had flowed into active pit #2 due to failure of the air actuator for active pit #2 and the suction valve for active pit #3. Poor communication between the Derrickman and Mud Loggers, combined with equipment failure and lack of continuous pit monitoring, resulted in the unauthorized discharge.
Recommendations:
Implement continuous monitoring of pit volumes during all fluid transfer operations
Establish robust communication protocols between Derrickman, Mud Loggers, and Driller
Conduct regular maintenance and testing of air actuators and suction valves
Require verification of pit contents before initiating transfers
Ensure personnel remain in the Mud Control Room during active pit operations

Top Event: Well Control Incident - Unintentional Shearing of Fluid Loss Control Devices
Incident Type: Underground blowout
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: completion fluid, packer fluid, crude oil, calcium carbonate
Summary: On January 9, 2021, at approximately 06:00 hours, Kosmos Energy experienced a well control incident on Seadrill West Neptune during completion operations on the SS003 well. While displacing wellbore completion fluid to packer fluid, the downhole Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) unintentionally sheared the upper and lower Fluid Loss Control Devices (FLCD), resulting in cross flow between completed intervals and fluid losses of 600 barrels per hour. The well was subsequently shut in and killed over a multi-day operation. The incident was attributed to inadequate risk recognition, poor communication, insufficient execution and supervision, and incomplete procedures.
Recommendations:
Improve risk assessment during planning and review phases of completion operations
Enhance procedural development to address risks associated with fluid loss control devices
Implement robust line-by-line procedural review and Complete Well on Paper (CWOP) processes
Establish direct communication protocols between Day Drilling Supervisor and Lead Drilling Supervisor
Require on-site presence of Halliburton Tool Representative and Consultant Completion Specialist during critical operations
Include specific pump rate and pump pressure parameters in displacement procedures
Implement Stop Work procedures when critical information is not provided
Enhance supervision and direct instruction during critical drilling operations
Develop detailed Plan of Action (POA) with measurable parameters for critical steps

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge to Sea
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, workover fluids
Summary: On 13 July 2020 at 1055 hours, a pollution event occurred at BP's Thunder Horse platform (MC 778-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. While ramping up production following pigging and flowback operations, production operators observed a sheen covering 90% of a 100-yard area. BP's failure to adequately flush workover fluids from subsea well MC 822 TD003 S45 before placing it into production resulted in abnormal emulsion and foaming in process vessels. This caused an estimated 3 barrels of oil to be discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the overboard produced water handling system. The incident was not reported to the National Response Center or BSEE until 23 July 2020, 10 days after occurrence, violating reporting requirements.
Recommendations:
NOD office recommends OII coordinate with BSEE Office of Environmental Compliance/Environmental Monitoring Unit and Oil Spill Preparedness Division to determine agency response to ongoing sheen events and oil and grease limit exceedances for Thunder Horse facility

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Gas Release
Incident Type: Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On 2 August 2020 at 1925 hours, BP production operators were preparing to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher KAH-4190 at Thunder Horse platform in the Gulf of Mexico. When operators began cracking open the launcher door, a gas cloud was observed emanating from the top of the launcher. The operators immediately shut the door. The combustible gas detector ASH 962-102, located 3 feet above the launcher, activated and reached 88% of the Low Explosive Limits (LEL). The gas cloud dissipated to 0% LEL within 2 minutes. The incident was caused by oil residue in the pig trap generating a hazardous atmosphere after the launcher had been isolated for 5 days. The launcher was not purged with nitrogen immediately before opening the door, contrary to standard operating procedures. All safety systems responded as designed and no injuries occurred.
Recommendations:
Ensure proper procedures are followed which include purging the trap with nitrogen before opening the pig launcher door

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Produced Water Discharge
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, water clarifier chemical
Summary: A pollution event occurred on 4 May 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when approximately 5 barrels of hydrocarbons were released into the Gulf of Mexico from the produced water outfall. The incident was caused by operator error leaving the water clarifier injection quill to Float Cell #2 open, allowing 286 gallons of water clarifier to gravity drain into the vessel. This excess clarifier caused a lack of oil/water separation. The issue was prolonged because a faulty level control valve in Float Cell #1 prevented switching to the backup produced water train. The sheen was first detected via satellite imagery on 4 May 2021 and the discharge continued through 9 May 2021.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Firewater system piping rupture and dropped object
Incident Type: Equipment failure and structural damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: seawater, diesel fuel, Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF)
Summary: On 09 May 2025 at 1530 hours, an Emergency Shutdown event occurred at MC 127 A SPAR platform when EnerMech technicians accidentally initiated a pressure loss in the Temperature Safety Element loop while troubleshooting an air compressor. During recovery from the ESD, multiple procedural failures and equipment issues led to air entering the firewater system. When the Fire Water Pumps were restarted, a water hammer effect ruptured multiple sections of FiberBond firewater piping, causing extensive damage. A metal support clamp weighing 1.5 pounds fell 30 feet from the ruptured piping and landed 5-6 feet from a person near Lifeboat 4. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.
Recommendations:
Update Power Generation System Procedure to include opening SDV 2082 after ESD events
Revise Firewater System Operating Procedure to reference correct valve (PCV 6650) instead of non-existent Sea Chest Valve
Update Power Protection and Life Support Systems Procedures to reflect current JWP equipment
Implement requirement to open helideck hose reels to bleed air from firewater system before starting FWPs
Inspect and repair or replace leaking check valve FSV 6650-004
Review all operating procedures to ensure alignment with current platform design
Add equipment and replace faulty equipment as identified

A metal support clamp weighing 1.5 pounds fell 30 feet from the ruptured piping and landed 5-6 feet from a person near Lifeboat 4. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.
recommendations: ["Update Power Generation System Procedure to include opening SDV 2082 after ESD events", "Revise Firewater System Operating Procedure to reference correct valve (PCV 6650) instead of non-existent Sea Chest Valve", "Update Power Protection and Life Support Systems Procedures to reflect current JWP equipment", "Implement requirement to open helideck hose reels to bleed air from firewater system before starting FWPs", "Inspect and repair or replace leaking check valve FSV 6650-004", "Review all operating procedures to ensure alignment with current platform design", "Add equipment and replace faulty equipment as identified"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Pollution event - discharge of oil into Gulf of America waters
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, emulsion breaker chemical, oil and grease
Summary: On 08 February 2025, BP personnel at Na Kika platform began injecting a trial emulsion breaker chemical into the produced water treatment system. The new chemical caused frothing and foaming within production vessels, leading to faulty level readings and liquid carryover. This resulted in approximately 89 barrels of oil and grease discharging from the produced water overboard system into the Gulf of America from 08-15 February 2025. The discharge went undetected for 7 days due to the discharge point being located 80 feet below the water surface. Once BP identified the chemical trial as the cause, they discontinued the trial and returned to the original formula, successfully ending the pollution event.
Recommendations:
BSEE Production Operations Support Section should reach out to industry to require additional safeguards to detect system upsets more quickly
Develop alternative methods to detect pollution events besides visual observation from the platform, given the discharge point location 80 feet below water surface
Improve monitoring systems to enable faster identification of unfavorable chemical trial results

Operating Phase: production

Summary: On 24 December 2023 at 0743 hours, a fire occurred on a rental air compressor being used for painting and blasting operations on the FPU 'Who Dat' in MC 547 A, approximately 39 miles offshore in 3280 feet of water. The fire was caused by failure of a 90° elbow on the oil separator piping, which allowed oil to spray into the enclosure and ignite on hot surfaces. The platform's fire detection system and Emergency Shut Down system functioned as designed. The fire was safely extinguished by the platform's Fire Team using water. No injuries, environmental impact, or platform damage occurred. Damage was limited to the rental air compressor and minor charring of adjacent generator enclosure wiring. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Well Leak
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid, methanol, calcium bromide completion brine, synthetic based oil
Summary: On January 6, 2023, at 13:00 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. experienced a pollution event at Mississippi Canyon 943 where subsea well PN003-ST2-BP1 released approximately 16.61 barrels of fluid mixture (hydraulic fluid, methanol, calcium bromide completion brine, and synthetic based oil) to the environment. The leak originated from loss of integrity in the Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) control lines, with fluid being released through the High Pressure vent on the subsea tree. The leak was detected and stopped by closing control valves SV1 and SV2 via ROV. The well was secured with 2 barriers and remains shut in with continuous pressure monitoring.
Recommendations:
Tubing to be pulled and analyzed to identify specific causes of failure
New tubing to be re-run before bringing well back online
BSEE to engage with Shell's analysis to inform policy and procedure to prevent future reoccurrence

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Hydrocarbon Release
Incident Type: hydrocarbon release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrocarbon fluid
Summary: On 10 May 2023 at approximately 0930 hours, two workers employed by Grand Isle Shipyard were removing bolts from a flange on a 2-inch Well Equalization Header line during a scheduled shut-in at MC 941 Platform A (Titan). As the workers loosened the last bolt, trapped pressure within the line escaped and sprayed the two workers in the face with a small amount of hydrocarbon fluid. The release activated a nearby gas detector, sounding the platform alarm. No injuries, pollution, or equipment damage occurred. The incident was caused by oversight of residual fluids and trapped pressure in a dog-leg bend above the flange during the isolation process.
Recommendations:
Review isolations to look for any possibility of hydrocarbon release from dog-legs or dead-legs due to residual fluid static pressure remaining after lockout/tagout is completed, even if zero pressure is indicated on the gauge
Implement engineering controls for residual fluids or static pressure release
Ensure personnel review isolations for potential hydrocarbon release from dog-legs or dead-legs even if gauge indicates zero pressure

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea MEG Leak
Incident Type: Leak
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Mono Ethylene Glycol (MEG)
Summary: On July 11, 2018, at 1200 hours, an estimated 17,098 gallons of Mono Ethylene Glycol (MEG) was discharged subsea from the Na Kika Platform (MC 474-A) following repair work on PZZ 2591/92 MEG pumps. The leak occurred through leaking Automatic Operated Valve (AOV) and Shut Down Valve (SDV) into the EA2 umbilical circuit. The leak went undetected for approximately 4 days (July 11-15, 2018) due to subsea location and pollutant dissipation before reaching the surface. The incident resulted in an unauthorized discharge violation and failure to notify BSEE.
Recommendations:
Perform survey to determine whether any other out-of-service (OOS) lines should be positively isolated or documented
Positively isolate the EA-2 MEG line
Consider removing SDV from Sempcheck and safe charts
Include topsides interfaces for hydraulics, chemical services, and HMI in well P&A plans
Confirm no metallurgy issues for other subsea kit currently in service
Consider monitoring methods for earlier detection including real-time unallocated MEG calculation displayed on HMI
Survey existing services shared with multiple wells for passing AOVs and SDVs and ensure proper labeling and recording

Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM)
Summary: On 1-Dec-2018 at 03:35 hours, the drillship Pacific Sharav experienced extreme unexpected changes in wind speed and direction due to strong thunderstorms in Mississippi Canyon Block 607, causing a forced vessel excursion. The vessel crossed the Dynamic Positioning Red Alert threshold at 03:38 hours. At 03:39 hours, the Senior Toolpusher manually activated the Emergency Disconnect Sequence (EDS-3), which actuated the Blowout Preventer Casing Shear Rams and sheared the drill pipe. The LMRP was disconnected from the BOP at 03:40 hours, resulting in the release of 1,673 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud into Gulf of Mexico waters. The well was secured with no indication of flow verified by ROV at 06:18 hours.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, methanol, Low-Dose Hydrate Inhibitor (LDHI), produced water
Summary: On May 16, 2018, a pollution event occurred at Noble Energy's Mississippi Canyon 736 A (Thunder Hawk) Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident involved the discharge of approximately 14.77 barrels of oil from the facility's overboard water line. The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.
Recommendations:
Update start-up procedures and quality control checks to ensure proper sequencing of subsea well ramp-ups to account for large volumes of chemicals
Increase communication between offshore operations and onshore production leadership
Review and revise produced water handling system procedures to account for increased water production
Implement proper sequencing of well startups from unplanned shutdowns

Summary: A pollution event occurred on 7 June 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when attempting to bring the S5 well online from a long-term shut-in. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Inadvertent mixing of sulfuric acid and sodium hypochlorite resulting in chlorine gas release
Incident Type: Chemical reaction and toxic gas release
Operating Phase: chemical unloading
Materials: sulfuric acid (30%), sodium hypochlorite (12.5%), propylene oxide, sodium hydroxide, acetic anhydride
Summary: On October 21, 2016, at approximately 7:35 AM, a Harcros Chemicals cargo tank motor vehicle arrived at MGPI Processing facility in Atchison, Kansas to deliver 30% sulfuric acid. During unloading operations, the driver inadvertently connected the sulfuric acid discharge hose to the unsecured sodium hypochlorite fill line instead of the sulfuric acid fill line. Approximately 4,000 gallons of sulfuric acid mixed with 5,850 gallons of sodium hypochlorite in the bulk tank, initiating a highly exothermic reaction that produced a greenish-yellow toxic gas cloud containing chlorine and other compounds. The cloud migrated northeast from the facility, impacting workers onsite and community members. Over 140 individuals sought medical attention; one MGPI employee and five community members were hospitalized. The incident was mitigated by emergency responders closing the cargo tank discharge valve and activating the facility's deluge system.
Recommendations:
Design chemical transfer equipment with physical separation between incompatible fill lines
Implement uniquely shaped and color-coded hose couplings for each chemical or class of chemicals
Install automated or remote emergency shut-off systems for chemical unloading operations
Improve pipe markings and labeling to clearly identify chemical lines
Enhance chemical unloading procedures to include verification steps and secondary confirmation
Ensure emergency escape respirator face pieces are stored in readily accessible locations
Conduct comprehensive process hazard analysis (PHA) considering human factors
Implement management of change procedures for equipment modifications
Provide training to drivers and operators on chemical incompatibilities and safe unloading practices
Establish communication protocols between facility operators and delivery drivers

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion of pressurized vessel during chemical treatment and draining operations
Incident Type: Reactive chemical explosion
Operating Phase: decommissioning and chemical treatment
Materials: mercaptan (tert-butyl mercaptan, ethyl methyl sulfide), sodium hypochlorite, natural gas, aluminum, methanol, hydrogen gas
Summary: Two explosions occurred at Midland Resource Recovery facility in Philippi, West Virginia on May 24, 2017 and June 20, 2017. The May 24 incident killed two workers (including company founder/president) and severely injured another. The June 20 incident fatally injured a contractor from Specialized Professional Services, Inc. Both explosions occurred during draining of Peerless MP odorizers that had been chemically treated with sodium hypochlorite. The probable cause was reactive, unstable chemicals generated during treatment of uncharacterized liquid residues in the vessels. MRR lacked effective process safety management systems to identify and control hazards from reactive chemicals.
Recommendations:
OSHA should establish or revise standards to require companies dealing with reactive chemistry to conduct formal hazard analyses and implement robust safety management systems
EPA should strengthen Risk Management Plan (RMP) regulations to address reactive chemical hazards
Companies should avoid treating uncharacterized waste materials with sodium hypochlorite due to explosive hazard potential
Companies should conduct thorough evaluation of reactive chemistry under design and abnormal conditions
Implement formal hazard identification processes before decommissioning equipment
Install pressure relief protection on vessels containing reactive chemicals
Establish complete chemical characterization protocols before treatment operations

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Anhydrous Ammonia Release
Incident Type: Catastrophic piping failure due to hydraulic shock
Operating Phase: production
Materials: anhydrous ammonia
Summary: On August 23, 2010, a hydraulic shock event caused catastrophic failure of a 12-inch roof-mounted suction pipe at Millard Refrigerated Services, releasing 32,100 pounds of anhydrous ammonia. The incident was triggered by a 7-hour power outage followed by manual interruption of a defrost cycle, which bypassed the programmed bleed sequence. When the system restarted, high-pressure hot gas mixed with low-temperature liquid in the suction line, causing condensation-induced shock and vapor-propelled liquid phenomena that ruptured both the roof piping and an evaporator coil. The ammonia cloud traveled 0.25 miles across Theodore Industrial Canal, exposing 153 people including 1 Millard employee, 9 ship crew members, and 143 offsite contractors. Of those exposed, 32 were hospitalized and 4 placed in intensive care.
Recommendations:
Avoid grouping multiple large-capacity evaporators to a single set of control valves; use individual valve control groups for each evaporator
Program defrost control systems to automatically depressurize or bleed coils upon restart after power outage or interruption prior to opening suction stop valve
Avoid manual interruption of evaporators during defrost; equip control systems with password-protected controls to restrict access to trained and authorized personnel only
Ensure pump-out times are long enough to remove residual liquid refrigerant from evaporator coils prior to introducing hot gas, especially after low-load periods or power outages
In event of ammonia release that cannot be promptly isolated, activate emergency shut-down switch to de-energize pumps, compressors and valves instead of attempting to isolate leaking equipment while system is running

Top Event: Explosion of blasting cap during testing
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: blasting cap, detonator
Summary: On September 22, 2019, at 06:55 hours, an Electric Line Operator (ELO) at McMoRan's Main Pass 299 A facility sustained puncture wounds to his right-hand fingers, left forearm, and left torso when a Green Detonator blasting cap exploded during continuity testing. The ELO used an incorrect testing device (megohmmeter) instead of the proper galvanometer, and did not place the blasting cap inside the safety loading tube prior to testing. The incident resulted in the ELO being transported by helicopter for medical evaluation, which determined that only first aid was required.
Recommendations:
A Safety Alert is forthcoming
Ensure proper tool selection and labeling for explosive testing equipment
Implement mandatory explosive safety training for all personnel handling explosives
Ensure blasting caps are tested inside protective shrouds
Ensure job safety analysis documents include all explosive-related hazards and tasks

Top Event: Fire in compressor-generator building
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, lube oil
Summary: On 21 September 2021 at approximately 1550 hours, an Emergency Shut Down occurred at MP 42 D/L/M facility. A leak in the ESD/TSE loop caused loss of main power and rendered both primary and secondary seal oil pumps inoperable. Combined with a liquid carryover event in the first stage suction scrubber, produced gas bypassed the wet seals and entered the lube oil tank. The overpressurized tank lid blew off, spraying lube oil and natural gas onto the hot turbine engine, causing ignition and rapid fire spread. All personnel were safely evacuated with no injuries or pollution.

N/A

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Nitrogen bottle explosion
Incident Type: explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: nitrogen
Summary: On 27 April 2025 at 1520 hours, a 12-bottle nitrogen rack on the top deck of MP 42 Delta platform experienced an explosion. One nitrogen bottle with corrosion at its base ruptured, causing the 'daisy-chained' tubing and isolation valves to be ripped from the remaining bottles, launching them across the platform. Four bottles went overboard into the Gulf; seven were scattered on deck. One bottle remained severely damaged in the aluminum rack. No personnel were injured. Damage included the aluminum rack, welding machine door, and section of solid decking. The bottles were at least 1 year overdue for inspection.
Recommendations:
Implement routine inspection and maintenance program for nitrogen bottles used offshore
Perform proper hazard analysis for nitrogen bottle storage and use
Inspect all nitrogen cylinders on platforms across the Gulf to identify similar deficiencies
Ensure nitrogen bottles are inspected on schedule to prevent corrosion-related failures

Summary: On July 4, 2014 at 1300 hours, construction workers on the main deck heard an unusual noise from the cellar deck. These valve deficiencies had been previously identified on August 16, 2013 but were not mitigated by management. The facility was evacuated and the well was isolated.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Rotary dryer over-pressurization and explosion
Incident Type: Chemical decomposition reaction and explosion
Operating Phase: toll manufacturing / dehydration
Materials: sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (NaDCC dihydrate, CDB-56®), sodium dichloroisocyanurate (NaDCC, CDB-63®), chlorine gas, nitrogen trichloride, methanol
Summary: On December 8, 2020, at approximately 10:00 p.m., a pressure-rated rotary double cone dryer containing sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (NaDCC dihydrate, CDB-56®) exploded at Optima Belle LLC in Belle, West Virginia. The explosion occurred during a toll manufacturing operation in which Optima Belle was dehydrating CDB-56® to produce anhydrous sodium dichloroisocyanurate (CDB-63®) on behalf of Clearon Corporation through a contractual agreement with tolling outsourcing partner Richman Chemical Inc. (RCI). The chlorinated isocyanurate compound underwent an unexpected self-accelerating decomposition reaction, releasing gases that increased the dryer internal pressure above its design pressure, causing the dryer to explode. Metal debris and dryer fragments propelled off-site and within the facility, striking a methanol pipe that subsequently caught fire. One Optima Belle employee (John Mark Gillenwater II) was fatally injured, two others were evaluated for respiratory irritation, and one Kanawha County resident reported a minor leg injury. Debris was found almost a half mile from the site. Estimated property damage was $33.1 million.
Recommendations:
Optima Belle: Develop and implement a written thermal and reactive hazards evaluation and management program adhering to CCPS guidance (2021-02-I-WV-R1)
Optima Belle: Develop and implement a written program for tolling process design and equipment selection using CCPS guidance to ensure equipment design basis is adequate, safeguards are properly designed, and new processes are evaluated at laboratory and pilot scales before production scale (2021-02-I-WV-R2)
Optima Belle: Develop and implement a formalized program for toll manufacturing agreements using CCPS resources to identify roles and responsibilities, evaluate equipment requirements, and ensure participation by all parties (2021-02-I-WV-R3)
Optima Belle: Develop and implement a process safety management system consistent with CCPS guidance addressing hazard identification, risk analysis, and management of risk (2021-02-I-WV-R4)
Clearon: Develop and implement a comprehensive process knowledge management program ensuring robust process technology and safety information packages, awareness of critical reactive chemistry information, and procedures for transmittal to toll manufacturers (2021-02-I-WV-R5)
Clearon: Update the sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (CDB-56®) safety data sheet to provide reasoning for storage and decomposition temperatures, explain differences between decomposition temperature and lowest temperature for self-accelerating decomposition, and provide exothermic decomposition energy (2021-02-I-WV-R6)
Clearon: Develop and implement a written program for tolling process design and equipment selection using CCPS guidance (2021-02-I-WV-R7)
Clearon: Develop and implement a formalized program for toll manufacturing agreements using CCPS resources (2021-02-I-WV-R8)
Clearon: Develop and implement a process safety management system consistent with CCPS guidance (2021-02-I-WV-R9)
RCI: Develop and implement a formalized program for toll manufacturing agreements using CCPS resources to identify roles and responsibilities and ensure participation by all parties (2021-02-I-WV-R10)
OSHA: Update the Chemical Reactivity Hazards website to include various reactivity assessment tools developed since 2002 (2021-02-I-WV-R11)
OSHA: Develop and implement a comprehensive outreach plan to ensure chemical industry awareness of the Chemical Reactivity Hazards website (2021-02-I-WV-R12)
OSHA: Amend the Process Safety Management (PSM) Standard, 29 CFR 1910.119, to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards by broadening application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, requiring consultation of multiple information sources, and augmenting process hazard analysis to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards (2021-02-I-WV-R13)
NCBI: Update safety information in PubChem for sodium dichloroisocyanurate (NaDCC) dihydrate to include reactivity and decomposition information including SADT, explosion hazard, and DSC and ARC results (2021-02-I-WV-R14)
CCPS: Update Guidelines for Process Safety in Outsourced Manufacturing Operations or develop new tolling guidance document to include current best practices, guidance specific to tolling brokers, and alignment with comprehensive management systems framework (2021-02-I-WV-R15)

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Foul condensate tank explosion
Incident Type: Vapor space explosion
Operating Phase: maintenance outage
Materials: non-condensable gas, turpentine, methanol, hydrogen sulfide, methyl mercaptan, dimethyl sulfide, dimethyl disulfide, water
Summary: On February 8, 2017, at approximately 11:05 am CDT, a foul condensate tank at the PCA DeRidder containerboard mill exploded during annual maintenance outage. The tank, part of a non-condensable gas system, contained water with a layer of flammable turpentine and an explosive vapor space. Air entered the tank through a pressure vacuum breaker during normal pressure swings and cooling, mixing with turpentine vapor to create an explosive atmosphere. Hot work (welding) on water piping above the tank likely ignited the explosive mixture. The explosion killed three contractors and injured seven others, launching the 100,000-gallon tank approximately 375 feet over a six-story building. The incident resulted from inadequate process hazard analysis, ineffective safeguards, and failure to apply process safety management systems to the non-condensable gas system.
Recommendations:
Evaluate and replace foul condensate tank with vessel designed for full-vacuum conditions
Install explosion prevention systems on non-condensable gas equipment
Design foul condensate tank with weak roof-to-shell attachment (frangible roof)
Implement robust administrative controls for non-condensable gas system operations
Conduct process hazard analysis for non-condensable gas system
Expand process safety management program boundaries beyond regulatory requirements
Apply hierarchy of controls to non-condensable gas system safeguards
Evaluate inherently safer design options to eliminate air ingress pathways
Establish clear ownership and responsibility for foul condensate tank operations
Apply TAPPI TIP 0416-09, NFPA 69, NFPA 68, and ISA 84 standards to non-condensable gas systems
Reiterate OSHA Process Safety Management standard coverage to include atmospheric storage tanks

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pipeline Rupture
Incident Type: Pollution - Leak
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 30 June 2022 at 2346 hrs, while conducting subsea pipeline modification operations on PSN 11015 in the Gulf of Mexico (205 ft water depth, 41 miles offshore), a diamond wire saw cut through an 18-inch diameter pipeline. At approximately 65% completion of the cut, the pipeline suddenly separated due to unknown stored energy, causing each end to move in opposite directions. This unexpected torsion/tension caused the temporary 8-inch bypass piping to shear at a flange connection, releasing approximately 90 barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over a 3-day period. The incident was caused by personnel not being aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline, and was contributed to by lack of communication with a pipeline contributor prior to performing the cut.
Recommendations:
All available historical information will be thoroughly reviewed to determine if potential for increase in stored energy exists on Third Coast pipelines under construction
Additional control measures will be implemented to mitigate substantial movement of pipeline prior to commencing construction work

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Rupture of steel pipe elbow with release of hydrofluoric acid and hydrocarbons
Incident Type: Loss of Containment with Fire and Explosions
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrofluoric acid, propane, butylene, isobutane, butane, propylene
Summary: On June 21, 2019 at 4:00:16 a.m., a steel pipe elbow in the HF alkylation unit ruptured after the control room operator increased feed to the T-7 propane stripper. The elbow, containing high concentrations of nickel and copper, had corroded from HF exposure and thinned to 0.011 inch (less than 7% of retirement thickness). The rupture released approximately 95% propane, 2.5% HF, and other hydrocarbons, forming a large vapor cloud. The cloud ignited two minutes later at 4:02:06 a.m. The RAD system was successfully activated, draining 339,000 pounds of HF. However, the water mitigation system control communication failed at ignition, and backup power failed nine seconds later, preventing remote activation of water spray cannons. Three secondary explosions occurred, with the largest at 4:22 a.m. when vessel V-1 ruptured, ejecting a 38,000-pound fragment across the Schuylkill River. Manual activation of the water pump at 4:39 a.m. allowed HF mitigation spraying. The fire was extinguished on June 22. Total HF release was estimated at 5,239 pounds, with 3,271 pounds released to atmosphere. Approximately 676,000 pounds of hydrocarbons released, 608,000 pounds combusted. Property damage estimated at $750 million, ranking as the third-largest refinery loss worldwide since 1974. Five workers and one firefighter sustained minor injuries. No off-site fatalities reported.
Recommendations:
EPA: Develop program prioritizing inspections of refinery HF alkylation units under National Compliance Initiative; verify compliance with API RP 751 special emphasis inspection program for accelerated corrosion; protect safety-critical safeguards from fire and explosion hazards; install remotely-operated emergency isolation valves on HF and hydrocarbon vessels
EPA: Revise 40 C.F.R. Part 68 to require petroleum refineries with HF alkylation units to conduct safer technology and alternatives analysis (STAA) every 5 years
EPA: Initiate prioritization of hydrofluoric acid as High-Priority Substance under TSCA and conduct risk evaluation
API: Update API RP 751 to require protection of critical safeguards and control system components from fire and explosion hazards; require remotely-operated emergency isolation valves on HF and hydrocarbon vessels
ASTM International: Revise ASTM A234 to incorporate supplementary requirements for piping in HF service as defined in ASTM A106 version 19a

Top Event: Fire and overpressure in Heater Treater burner system
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, fuel oil
Summary: On September 23, 2021 at 1010 hours, a Burner Safety Low alarm occurred on the NBK-2900 Heater Treater at Talos Energy's South Pelto Platform. Two operators responded: one at the burner location and one at the master panel. After resetting the fuel shutdown valve relay and relighting the burners, a pressure release occurred when the West side burner was lit. The root cause was identified as restricted airflow in the stack arrestor from a previous fire tube fire on August 1, 2021, combined with insufficient purge time of residual gas before reignition. A second incident occurred on September 30, 2021 during restart, revealing charred wiring on the igniter and ground wires. The burner section was permanently taken out of service.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in Heater Treater Fire Tube
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, CO2, phosphoric acid, paraffin inhibitor, emulsion breaker
Summary: On 29-FEB-2016 at 1930 hours, a fire occurred inside the 24-inch fire tube of the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) on the South Pelto PL 23-D platform. The fire was caused by thermo-galvanic corrosion of the A-106 Grade B schedule 20 fire tube material. Multiple corrosion-accelerating parameters were present: low pH produced water (2.95), high chloride concentration (25,028 mg/l), temperature gradient on the metal surface, and scale deposition. The phosphoric acid in the emulsion breaker contributed to the low pH. The calculated pitting corrosion rate was 450 mils/year, an extremely high rate requiring thermo-galvanic corrosion as the driving force. The fire was quickly extinguished with no injuries or evacuation. The platform remained shut in for repairs until 18-MAR-2016.
Recommendations:
Add anodes for cathodic protection of shell and fire tube
Construct new fire tube with heat resistant coating
Change chemical program to use emulsion breaker that will not produce calcium phosphate scale
Implement scale inhibitor program to address calcium carbonate scale deposition
Reduce fluid temperature in treater from 120°F to 105°F
Pull and visually inspect fire tube at 60 days, 180 days, and establish ongoing inspection interval based on corrosion rates
Generate and distribute lessons learned to all platforms

Top Event: Hydrogen Sulfide Release
Incident Type: H2S release and muster
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: crude oil, condensate, water, hydrogen sulfide
Summary: At approximately 1730 hours on 25-June-2018, an H2S release occurred on the South Pelto 5-B platform during abandonment operations involving flushing of eight platforms and fifteen pipelines. A worker detected an odor of H2S at an open top flow back tank. An H2S detector recorded 100 ppm of gas. All operations were stopped and personnel mustered for safety. H2S readings steadily decreased from 100 ppm to 0 ppm over nine hours. No personnel were injured. Investigation determined that water left in pipelines for over two years prior to abandonment operations allowed high concentrations of bacteria growth, which led to small pockets of H2S gas formation.

N/A

Top Event: Gas well blowout and rig fire
Incident Type: Blowout with fire
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: natural gas, diesel oil-based drilling mud
Summary: On January 22, 2018, a blowout and rig fire occurred at Pryor Trust Well 1H-9 in Pittsburg County, Oklahoma. The fire killed five workers inside the driller's cabin from thermal burn injuries and smoke/soot inhalation. The blowout occurred approximately 3.5 hours after tripping drill pipe out of the well. Root causes included failure of the primary barrier (hydrostatic pressure from drilling mud) and secondary barrier (human detection of influx and BOP activation). Contributing factors included underbalanced operations performed without proper planning or equipment, inadequate training on electronic trip sheets, equipment misalignment causing data interpretation errors, failure to identify surface pressure, miscalculated weighted pill, deactivated alarm system, lack of flow checks, inadequate driller training in influx detection, and ineffective safety management system. The BOP failed to close after the fire because hydraulic control hoses burned and leaked, depleting accumulator pressure.
Recommendations:
OSHA should establish drilling-specific regulatory standards for onshore drilling safety
API should develop industry standards for alarm management in drilling operations
API should develop industry guidance on tripping procedures for underbalanced operations
Patterson-UTI should implement effective monitoring of policy implementation including flow checks
Patterson-UTI should test drillers' influx detection skills through simulated pit gains
Patterson-UTI should improve driller training on electronic trip sheets
Red Mountain Operating should specify required barriers during operations and response procedures
IADC should develop tripping guidance for underbalanced operations
Pason Systems should improve alarm system design for different operating states
NOV should improve BOP design and hydraulic line protection
State of Oklahoma should establish drilling safety regulations

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Well Leak
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 8 May 2022 at 1130 hours, a sheen of unknown origin was observed on the water at Mississippi Canyon Block 940, OCS-G 31534. The leak originated from Subsea Well MC 940 (Vito) VA007, a completed well not yet tied back via pipeline to Shell's Vito facility. The source was identified as a tubing string parting at a coupling in the 5.5" tubing at approximately 5,122 feet below mudline. The leak was stopped within 4 days by closing the SV2 needle valve on the subsea tree. An estimated 7 barrels of oil were released into the Gulf of Mexico between 8 May and 12 May 2022. Shell's response and reporting were found to be in compliance with federal regulations.
Recommendations:
BSEE will engage with Shell's analysis of tubing failure to inform policy and procedure to prevent future occurrence

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Pipe rupture and explosion
Incident Type: Loss of Containment - Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrogen, methane, hydrocarbons, hydrogen sulfide, crude oil
Summary: On November 4, 2009, a sudden and complete rupture occurred in the 10-inch diameter discharge line at the exit of the South Reactor (R30101) in the Mobil Distillate Dewaxing Unit (MDDW) during hot hydrogen catalyst regeneration. The rupture released approximately 175.3 kg of hydrogen-rich gas mixture at 642 psig and 785°F. The released gas ignited approximately 0.66 seconds after rupture, causing an explosion that damaged residential homes in the adjacent community. Metallurgical analysis revealed wall thinning from the inside diameter through the weld metal and base metal of the 10-inch straight segment, consistent with sulfidation corrosion. The piping had been in service since 1993 with no documented wall thickness monitoring between installation and the incident.
Recommendations:
Implement corrosion monitoring program for low-alloy carbon steel piping in sulfidation-prone service
Establish baseline wall thickness measurements and periodic ultrasonic inspection intervals
Consider material upgrades to molybdenum-containing alloys or stainless steel in high-temperature sulfur-containing service
Review process stream chemistry and operating conditions for sulfidation corrosion risk
Document original pipe specifications and maintain inspection records

Top Event: High-pressure fluid release causing facial injury
Incident Type: LTA (>3 days)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: gas lift fluid
Summary: On May 20, 2019 at approximately 1400 hours, an employee sustained injuries to his nose and face while attempting to close a 1/2" ball valve on the Gas Lift Fluid Slug Catcher (MBD-150) operating at 1200 psi. The employee inadvertently opened the valve, causing high-pressure release that detached the 3/8" drain tubing from a tie wrap. The tubing whipped and struck the employee in the face, causing a laceration to the bridge of the nose and a broken nose. The employee was wearing required PPE at the time.
Recommendations:
Issue Safety Alert concerning this incident
Remove the 3/8" tubing from the dump valve installation on the Gas Lift Fluid Slug Catcher to eliminate the hazard
Utilize a needle valve instead of a ball valve to eliminate the volume that caused the tubing to break free

Incident Type: Leak
Operating Phase: production
Materials: condensate
Summary: On December 3, 2014, a sheen was reported in the vicinity of South Marsh Island Area Block 27. Pipeline Segment Number 3334 operated by Sea Robin Pipeline Company was identified as the source. A hole measuring 1/2" by 3/4" was located at the 6 o'clock position at the bottom of the line at 93 feet water depth. The spill volume was initially reported as 15 barrels but later revised to 0.7 barrels of condensate. The leak was secured on December 5, 2014, and repairs were completed by December 12, 2014. Investigation findings indicated no corrosion, no third-party damage, and normal cathodic protection readings, suggesting the probable cause was a manufacturing defect or isolated pit in the pipe.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire from compressor piston failure
Incident Type: Equipment failure with fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, compressed gas
Summary: On January 16, 2024, at 0252 hours, the C1 compressor at Arena Offshore's SM-128B facility experienced a piston failure. The piston rod nut loosened due to component fault or wear, causing the two bottom connecting rod bolts to break. This allowed the piston rod nut to contact the head end of the cylinder and separate from the frame. As the housing broke, gas ignited. The vibration switch detected the abnormal vibration and tripped, but failed to initiate shutdown at the panel due to moisture in the output line that froze in the 17°F ambient temperature. The night operator discovered the fire, activated the fire alarm and ESD, alerted personnel, and the fire was extinguished without injuries.
Recommendations:
Install in-line desiccant dryer to pneumatic supply inlets of compressor control panel to prevent condensation formation
Improve compressor maintenance procedures to prevent piston scarring and component wear
Ensure vibration switch signal integrity in cold weather conditions

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Pipeline rupture and fire
Incident Type: Equipment failure - severe external corrosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On December 15, 2023, at 0520 hours, an 8-inch bi-directional gas pipeline (Segment 5114) containing 1220 psi ruptured on the Talos ERT LLC SM 130 B facility in the Gulf of Mexico. The rupture resulted in a fire that traveled approximately 80 feet from below the +10 level to the production deck. A 3-foot section of pipeline from the +4 to +7 elevation was missing. The fire lasted 5-7 minutes before gas pressure bled down. Life saving equipment, wiring, and minor structural damage occurred near the +10 level. No injuries were reported. Favorable wind conditions (28 mph blowing away from platform) prevented more severe damage. Root cause was determined to be severe external corrosion of the pipeline flange driven by high chlorine concentrations (12,000 ppm) in an oxygen-containing environment, combined with inadequate maintenance of protective coating and failure to complete required mechanical integrity inspection.
Recommendations:
Complete mechanical integrity inspection of Segment 5114 with rope access near waterline
Implement preventative maintenance program for pipeline coatings
Ensure mechanical integrity tests are performed on required 5-year schedule

Top Event: Gas Release from Corroded Piping
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On 03 November 2025 at 1205 hours, a gas release was detected on SP 62 C platform during a BSEE annual production inspection. A BSEE Inspector observed suspected gas vapors coming from a corroded area on an 8-inch gas vent line. Platform operators confirmed the gas release using a portable gas detector. The platform was manually shut in and the corroded gas line was replaced. The incident resulted in no injuries or pollution. Root causes were identified as inadequate equipment inspection and inadequate equipment maintenance, with contributing factors related to inadequate job procedures for routine corrosion inspections.
Recommendations:
Establish routine corrosion inspections in addition to Level 1 surveys
Develop company-specific policy for routine corrosion inspections on process piping
Issue a Safety Alert regarding the incident
Increase attention to identifying corrosion issues on all platform process piping

Top Event: Fire in Heater Treater Fire Tube
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, production fluids, fuel gas, corrosive chemicals
Summary: On December 23, 2016, at approximately 1550 hours, personnel on the E-Auxiliary platform at Ship Shoal Block 154 noticed smoke coming from the exhaust stack of the Heater Treater (NBK-1800). After shutting off fuel gas, a small fire was observed inside the fire tube. The fire was extinguished with a handheld fire extinguisher. Investigation revealed corrosion and pitting in the welds of the fire tube, likely caused by caustic chemicals introduced into the production system that damaged the metal over time.
Recommendations:
Once the fire tube is pulled for inspection, it should be cleaned thoroughly, especially around welded areas and areas where fluid/chemical interface may occur, to determine whether corrosion or metal loss is an issue

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash fire during pipeline pump startup
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, fuel gas, crude oil
Summary: On March 7, 2025, at 08:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at W&T Offshore's production facility at Ship Shoal Block 80, Platform A while attempting to bring Pipeline Pump #2 (PAX-360) online. A W&T Mechanic and Contractor Electrician sustained burn injuries to head and hands. The fire resulted from a loose fitting on the fuel gas starter exhaust piping that allowed gas to accumulate near the pump. When the engine backfired during startup, the accumulated gas ignited. Both injured personnel were evacuated and treated; the Electrician was released to return to work on April 9, 2025, while the Mechanic continued treatment. The incident was exacerbated by personnel not wearing required PPE (gloves) and wind direction that pushed flames toward one worker.
Recommendations:
When using fuel gas to start prime movers, always ensure the starter exhaust gas is vented to a safe location
Review gas engine startup procedures to include equipment checks verifying proper installation of components
Utilize gas detectors when performing startup procedures on engines with gas as a fuel source and/or as a pneumatic supply for starters
Conduct mandatory checks on engines with gas as a fuel source and/or as a pneumatic supply for starters to ensure proper installation of starter exhaust piping
Replace all flexible hoses with hard piping for fuel gas systems
Conduct routine checks on all fittings associated with starter gas
Stress the importance of wearing all required PPE during equipment startup operations

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash fire from ignited gas during welding operations
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On May 17, 2014, at 0830 hours, while performing abandonment operations on Walter Oil & Gas's #001 well on the Rowan Gorilla IV, three employees were burned by a flash fire. The crew was welding pad eyes to the inside of a 36-inch casing to enable removal. Burning slag fell inside the wellbore, igniting gas that had migrated from the reservoir after the casing was cut. The three employees suffered first and second degree burns to their upper bodies. Investigation revealed gas was present at approximately 40 feet depth inside the casing but not at the surface where employees were working.
Recommendations:
Prior to performing any welding operations on casing, the operator should take measures to ensure that not only the immediate area is clear of all hydrocarbons, but that there is no gas present inside the casing as well.

Summary: At approximately 1200 hours on January 23, 2020, at South Timbalier Block 311 Platform A, an operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of an in-service LP Gas Compressor 3rd stage scrubber LSH with his foot/shin while exiting the area. This released approximately 1100 psi through a 3/8" stainless steel tubing drain line approximately 7 feet in length. The pressure release caused a whipping action in the drain tubing striking the operator on the side of the face/hardhat. Hydrocarbons from the 3rd stage suction scrubber vented toward the in-service LP gas compressor engine and ignited. Personnel activated the manual ESD station, signaled the fire alarm, and all personnel mustered. An attempt to use a nearby 125 lb. wheeled fire extinguisher unit failed to discharge. Fire water was used to extinguish the fire. Personnel reported first aid injuries (scraps, bumps, and skin redness). The fire caused damage to the compressor engine electrical components/wiring and the 3rd stage discharge PSV.

Fire water was used to extinguish the fire. Personnel reported first aid injuries (scraps, bumps, and skin redness). The fire caused damage to the compressor engine electrical components/wiring and the 3rd stage discharge PSV.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash Fire and Explosion
Incident Type: Hot work incident involving explosive atmosphere in piping
Operating Phase: maintenance
Materials: crude oil, butane, propane
Summary: On August 12, 2016, at approximately 8:15 PM CDT, a flash fire and explosion occurred while L-Con contractor was welding a piping segment at the Sunoco Nederland crude oil terminal. The 30-inch diameter piping section contained residual crude oil and was isolated using CARBER isolation tools. During internal welding of flange A, vapor inside the piping ignited, causing a pressure spike that ejected the isolation tools from both ends of the pipe. The explosion and flash fire injured seven contractors (four welders, one fire watch, two others), with four requiring hospitalization for burns, bone fractures, and blast-related injuries. The incident was caused by hot work being conducted on equipment containing an explosive atmosphere without proper clearing or inerting of flammable material.
Recommendations:
Equipment containing flammable materials must be purged, made safe, or decontaminated prior to hot work
Hot work activities must not proceed if flammable vapors are present above 10% LEL
Continuous atmospheric monitoring for flammable gases is preferred during hot work
Alternative precautions such as inert gas purging must be established when piping cannot be fully decontaminated
Isolation devices must be continuously monitored until servicing work is completed
Formal JHA must be performed each time a piping mechanical seal device is applied
Mechanical seal devices should only be used after available means of vapor removal have been employed

Top Event: Catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E
Incident Type: Loss of Containment - Explosion and Fire
Operating Phase: startup
Materials: hydrogen, naphtha, carbon steel, stainless steel
Summary: On April 2, 2010, the Tesoro Anacortes Refinery experienced a catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E in the Naphtha Hydrotreater (NHT) unit. The carbon steel heat exchanger failed due to High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA), releasing highly flammable hydrogen and naphtha at temperatures exceeding 500°F. The release ignited, causing an explosion and fire that burned for over three hours. Seven Tesoro employees (one shift supervisor and six operators) working in the immediate vicinity were fatally injured. This was the largest fatal incident at a US petroleum refinery since the BP Texas City accident in March 2005.
Recommendations:
EPA should enforce inherently safer design principles in petroleum refineries
Washington State Legislature should strengthen PSM regulations and regulatory oversight
Washington State DOSH should enhance inspection programs and competency
API should revise RP 941 to include minimum requirements and accurate HTHA guidance
API should revise RP 581 to require verification of actual operating conditions
Tesoro should implement inherently safer design using high chromium steels
Tesoro should improve Process Hazard Analysis procedures and effectiveness
Tesoro should enhance control of nonroutine hazardous work
United Steelworkers should participate in process safety oversight

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Sulfuric Acid Release
Incident Type: Chemical Release / Worker Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: sulfuric acid, hydrocarbons, isobutane, light olefins, naphtha
Summary: On February 12, 2014, two Tesoro Martinez refinery employees suffered first and second degree chemical burns while working to return a sulfuric acid sampling system to service following nonroutine maintenance. Concentrated sulfuric acid sprayed the workers following a mechanical integrity failure of a ¾-inch tubing connector adjacent to the acid sampling station. The tubing came apart at the connector when operators opened a valve to return the acid sampling system to service. Acid continued to spray from a nearly 100,000-gallon acid settler for over two hours, resulting in the release of approximately 84,000 pounds of sulfuric acid. Both workers were transported to a burn center and neither returned to work for over 150 days. The incident was caused by insufficient tightening of the compression joint. The CSB investigation found that weak process safety culture at the refinery, including failure to implement inherently safer design, inadequate PPE availability, poor maintenance practices, and failure to learn from previous incidents, were causal to the incident.
Recommendations:
Implement inherently safer sulfuric acid sampling system design with closed-loop systems
Ensure all required PPE is available and compatible for workers
Strengthen process hazard analysis to adequately evaluate sampling system hazards
Implement robust leak-testing procedures for tubing following repairs
Improve maintenance program planning, documentation, and communication
Strengthen process safety culture through management commitment and worker engagement
Implement effective corrective actions from safety culture assessments
Improve communication and implementation of lessons learned from previous incidents
Enhance regulatory oversight through proactive and preventive inspections
Use leading and lagging process safety indicators to identify impending serious incidents

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Butadiene Release
Incident Type: Chemical Release / Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: butadiene, popcorn polymer, hazardous chemicals
Summary: A dangerous substance known as popcorn polymer accumulated in a temporary dead leg created when a process pump was taken out of service for maintenance at the Huntsman Chemical Plant in Port Neches, Texas. The popcorn polymer expanded and ruptured the piping, releasing butadiene. The butadiene release resulted in fires that burned for more than a month, causing $40 million in on-site property damage and $9 million in off-site property damage. Two CSB employees and a contractor reported minor injuries, and at least five local residents reported injuries. The incident led to a mandatory four-mile radius evacuation order affecting Port Neches, Groves, Nederland, and portions of Port Arthur.
Recommendations:
Ensure that piping associated with out-of-service equipment in high-purity butadiene service be flushed monthly
Identify, control, or prevent all potential temporary dead legs within the unit
Implement effective safety management systems to identify when safety-critical procedures cannot be conducted as intended

Top Event: Molten salt eruption and steam explosion
Incident Type: Explosion with fatal injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: sodium hydroxide, sodium nitrate, molten salt, water, stainless steel
Summary: On May 30, 2024, at 8:58 a.m., an explosion occurred at the TS USA liquid nitriding facility in Chattanooga, Tennessee, while employees were processing metal rollers with cavities. Water became trapped in a roller cavity due to a solidified salt plug blocking the drain hole. When the roller was reintroduced into the 800°F oxidizing salt bath, the trapped water boiled and rapidly expanded as steam, creating overpressure that ejected the roller's press fit end. The contact of water with the molten salt caused a steam explosion that erupted approximately 4,500 pounds of molten sodium hydroxide and sodium nitrate from the bath, engulfing a line operator and causing second- and third-degree chemical and thermal burns over 95 percent of his body. The operator died later that day at the hospital. Three other employees suffered minor burns. The eruption ignited multiple fires throughout the facility, resulting in approximately $1.3 million in property damage.
Recommendations:
Implement physical protective barriers around molten salt baths to isolate employees from hazardous releases
Develop comprehensive safety management system including hazard analysis program, written operating procedures, training program, and incident investigation program
Establish position with specific expertise in safety management systems at each TS USA facility
Include physical protective barriers as standard design for liquid nitriding processes
Develop and implement effective knowledge management program for sharing information across HEF Groupe organization
Develop and implement comprehensive corporate governance program with regular audits and accountability tracking

Top Event: Explosion and Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure and Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, water, natural gas, diesel
Summary: On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during a well cleanup and test operation. Two Production Technology & Services Inc. (PTS) workers sustained second-degree burns and required evacuation by USCG. The incident resulted from improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel tank allowing gas to escape, which accumulated near a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module failed to shut down the engine when it overran due to gas ingestion, causing ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank seals, and failure of the generator's automatic emergency shutdown system.
Recommendations:
BSEE Lake Charles District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a Safety Alert/Bulletin regarding this incident

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash fire during compressor maintenance
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, fuel gas
Summary: On 29-NOV-2020 at 0657 hours, a flash fire occurred during maintenance troubleshooting of a gas compressor engine (CBA-2030) at the West Delta Block 105 Platform E. A mechanic was troubleshooting a non-firing No. 6 left bank power cylinder. When the mechanic disconnected the secondary spark plug wire from the ignition coil, a spark was introduced into an area containing leaked fuel gas at explosive concentrations. The gas ignited, causing a flash fire. The root cause was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line that allowed gas to leak. The mechanic immediately activated the ESD system, descended the catwalk, and extinguished the fire with a fire extinguisher. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. The platform was shut in until repairs were completed and permission to restart was granted by BSEE on 01-DEC-2020. The probable cause was identified as inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), failure to use a gas detector, and failure to utilize a hot work permit.
Recommendations:
Utilize gas detector to confirm area is gas-free prior to troubleshooting suspected ignition-related issues where equipment is still running or online
Utilize hot work permit program when troubleshooting suspected ignition issues with fire watch present
Improve pre-job planning and discussion between mechanic and Person In Charge (PIC) signing the JSA
Educate maintenance personnel on risks of disconnecting ignition wires while equipment is running
Ensure JSA is task-specific and identifies all potential hazards including gas leaks and ignition sources

Top Event: Pipeline Rupture
Incident Type: External Damage - Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, hydrocarbon
Summary: On September 9, 2018, at 1130 hours, a trawling vessel snagged a bi-directional pipeline (Segment No. 15815) in the Gulf of Mexico, causing it to rupture and release approximately 0.8 gallons of hydrocarbon. The pipeline failed due to excessive stresses imposed when the trawler's net snagged on an 8-inch to 6-inch tie-in spool and dragged it until failure. The tie-in spool, installed in 2007 as part of a Right-of-Way modification, was not sufficiently buried or covered as required by regulations. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge to Gulf Waters
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water
Summary: On 21 September 2016 at 0828 hours, BSEE Inspectors discovered an oil sheen 7 miles long and 1 mile wide originating from WD 32 E Platform. Investigation revealed that the Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) failed due to a stuck internal pump shifting/slider valve, preventing oil transfer from the Floatation Cell (ABM-410). Oil level in the oil chamber rose to the level of the produced water side and flowed back into the overboard discharge chamber. The Fisher 2500 Level Controller maintained preset water discharge level, causing oil to discharge through the overboard water outlet. The Level Safety High (LSH 1) shut down device was not installed in the oil chamber as required, preventing automatic platform shut-in. Approximately 8.05 barrels of crude oil were discharged into Gulf waters. The unmanned facility was in-service and producing at the time of discovery.
Recommendations:
LSH 1 (Fisher 2100) shut down device has been installed and function tested in the oil chamber of the floatation cell to prevent carryover of produced oil back into the water chamber and activate system shut-in
BSEE rescinded the 3-times-per-week pollution inspection frequency and now requires daily pollution inspection for WD 32 DAE facility

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Gas leak from pipeline riser
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil, slop oil
Summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak was discovered on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759), approximately 5-7 feet above the water line on the WD 73AT platform. The platform's ESD was activated, shutting down the facility and sounding the alarm. All 26 personnel on board were accounted for and sheltered in place in the quarters building upwind from the leak. The pipeline was bled down over several hours. A light sheen was observed during overflight, later determined to originate from the slop oil tank rather than the pipeline. Investigation revealed inadequate preventative maintenance, failed repairs documented in a 2015 survey, and insufficient pipeline inspection methods.
Recommendations:
Implement adequate preventative maintenance programs for pipeline risers
Ensure documented completion of repairs identified in inspection surveys
Utilize intelligent Pipeline Inspection Gauge (PIG) with magnetic flux leakage, ultrasound, and caliper technology
Ensure protective coating is applied to pipeline risers at hanger locations
Conduct Level 1 surveys with sufficient examination methods beyond drone inspection

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Well Control
Incident Type: Uncontrolled release of well fluids
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, seawater, well fluids
Summary: On April 21, 2025, at 19:30 CDT, the WD 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. An uncontrolled release of approximately 20 barrels of well fluids occurred due to a leak at the starter head spool. The incident was triggered when the P&A crew pumped fluid down the tubing to assist pressure equalization across a downhole plug. Oil was observed leaking from between the starter head flange and the surface casing annulus. After approximately 5-10 minutes, the fluid flow reduced and stopped, but uncontrolled gas was then observed, prompting full platform evacuation. All 22 personnel were safely evacuated. The well was shut in by closing the crown valve and cutting the wireline. No injuries were reported.
Recommendations:
More rigorous pre-job planning
Inclusion of complete and accurate wellbore history in procedures
Implementation of structured hazard assessment process
Earlier implementation of engineering controls such as rubberized sealant at spooling head
Systematic contingency planning and information sharing

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Well Control / Blowout
Incident Type: Well Blowout and Fire
Operating Phase: workover
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, brine, treated water
Summary: On January 29, 2020, at approximately 3:00 p.m., the Daniel H. Wendland 1-H well in Burleson County, Texas experienced a loss of well control during a workover operation to replace the tubing head. The well blowout released oil and gas which ignited within seconds, resulting in a flash fire that fatally injured three contract workers and seriously injured a fourth. The incident resulted in an estimated $1 million in property damage. Root causes included inadequate well planning, ineffective well control barriers for underpressured reservoirs, and poor ignition source management.
Recommendations:
Chesapeake Operating, L.L.C. should develop or revise policies incorporating API RP 59 recommendations regarding well planning, specifically including well history review in conjunction with workover well control planning
American Petroleum Institute should publish focused guidance on workover operations including completed wells in underpressured reservoirs, clarify that stabbing valves should not be considered as well control barriers, and emphasize that every well should have two well control barriers with at least one preventative barrier
OSHA should remove the exemption for oil and gas drilling and well servicing from the Control of Hazardous Energy standard (29 CFR 1910.147)
OSHA should promulgate a new standard with prescriptive requirements for primary and secondary barriers, detailed written procedures, management of change requirements, risk assessments, RAGAGEP compliance, Well Construction Interface Documents, flow checks, employee participation, critical well information maintenance, and hazard analysis for onshore oil and gas drilling, production, and servicing/workover activities
