Top Event: Fire and Detonation of Ammonium Nitrate
Incident Type: Fire and Explosion
Operating Phase: storage and distribution
Materials: fertilizer grade ammonium nitrate (FGAN), anhydrous ammonia, potash, diammonium phosphate, ammonium sulfate, zinc sulfate
Summary: On April 17, 2013, at approximately 7:51 pm CDT, a fire and explosion occurred at the West Fertilizer Company facility in West, Texas. The facility stored between 40 and 60 tons of fertilizer grade ammonium nitrate (FGAN). The explosion fatally injured 12 emergency responders and 3 members of the public, with more than 260 injured. The blast completely destroyed the WFC facility and caused widespread damage to over 150 offsite buildings, including schools, a nursing home, and residential structures. The total insurance-related losses were estimated at approximately $230 million.
Recommendations:
Implement inherently safer technology and alternative FGAN formulations
Establish automatic sprinkler systems in FGAN storage facilities
Improve federal and state regulations for FGAN storage and handling
Enhance emergency response training and pre-incident planning for hazardous materials
Implement land use planning and zoning regulations to separate hazardous chemical storage from populated areas
Improve insurance coverage requirements and loss control inspections
Enhance firefighter training on FGAN hazards and response procedures

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Discharge of Synthetic Based Mud Overboard
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: exploration
Materials: Synthetic Based Mud (SBM), saltwater
Summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, a saltwater kick occurred during drilling of the 12-1/4 inch hole section at approximately 25,000 feet on the Diamond Offshore West Vela drillship. The kick caused Surface Back Pressure in the MPD system to exceed the 1,300 psi PRV setting. Approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud were discharged overboard via the MPD system Pressure Relief Valves. The well was successfully shut in and the kick was circulated out. The incident was attributed to human performance error in the decision to divert drilling fluid overboard rather than to the flowline as specified in the New Technology Application.
Recommendations:
MPD procedures and hole section influx management envelope matrix should include a differential flow threshold to inform the Driller how to respond when using MPD
When using PCVs for MPD, ensure modeling is performed to determine optimal PCV setpoints with down hole pumps on based on maximum kick intensity and maximum PCV setpoint based on required SBP for connections with down hole pumps off
Set maximum PCV pressure to protect against PRVs from opening based on a maximum flow rate for the selected setpoint
Review FMCA for plugging downstream of the MPD choke to confirm the correct PRV set point should limit system to pressure rating of equipment upstream of the choke
Review the latest version of the flow control software that responds to influxes in step changes and switches the MPD from Flow Control to SBP mode when the high-pressure limit is reached

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unintentional discharge of synthetic oil based mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM)
Summary: On December 11, 2014, the Noble Jim Day unintentionally discharged 142 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico during mud pit cleaning operations. The discharge resulted from valve misalignment during the transition between crew changes. Keys to the dump valves were passed between personnel without proper Lock-out/Tag-out documentation. The Derrickman, lacking knowledge of proper valve alignment and receiving poor communication and labeling, misaligned the valves and created a pathway for hydrocarbons to discharge overboard. The Pit Volume Totalizer alarm detected the loss after approximately 5 minutes, at which point the crew secured the pits and corrected the valve alignment.
Recommendations:
Improve valve labeling and identification on piping systems
Enhance Lock-out/Tag-out procedures and documentation requirements
Update system drawings to accurately reflect valve installation
Conduct pre-tour safety meetings for all crew transitions
Improve training on proper valve alignment procedures
Enhance communication protocols during critical operations
Ensure Job Safety Analysis (JSA) identifies environmental hazards

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Based Mud Discharge
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Based Mud
Summary: While performing drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk for Anadarko Petroleum Corporation, two incidents occurred in which Synthetic Based Mud was inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico. The first incident on July 4, 2015 released 2.5 barrels of SBM, and the second on August 8, 2015 released 3.3 barrels of SBM. Both incidents were caused by a malfunction in the rig's software for the Slip Joint Packer management system, combined with inadequate crew monitoring of packer pressures. The software logic automatically adjusted low alarm set points based on instrument air pressure fluctuations without crew knowledge, causing the packer system to vent mud when pressure dropped below the adjusted set point.
Recommendations:
Manufacturer to develop and install upgrade to packer management system logic to prevent automatic adjustment of low alarm set points without crew knowledge

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Vapor Cloud Explosion and Fire
Incident Type: Vessel Leak, Vapor Cloud Explosion, and Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: VM&P naphtha (solvent), alkyd resin, flammable liquids
Summary: On April 8, 2021, at approximately 12:02 a.m., a mixture of flammable naphtha solvent vapors and resin liquid became pressurized and released through the seal of a closed manway on Kettle 3 at the Yenkin-Majestic OPC Polymers resin plant in Columbus, Ohio. The release occurred after the kettle operator turned on the agitator, which caused rapid vaporization of solvent that had been added to the hot resin while the agitator was unexpectedly off. The flammable vapor cloud spread throughout the enclosed building and formed both inside and outside. Approximately two minutes later, at 12:04 a.m., the vapor cloud ignited, causing an explosion and large fire. One employee was fatally injured from thermal injuries and inhalation of combustion products. Eight other employees were transported to hospitals with injuries including third-degree burns and limb fractures, with one requiring leg amputation. The incident caused over $90 million in property damage.
Recommendations:
Update mechanical integrity procedures for all process vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service, including pressure vessels not exceeding 15 psig, to adopt alteration guidance in API 510 or National Board Inspection Code Part 3
Assess and document applicable design, construction, and alteration standards for all pressure vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service, adopting PIP VESLP001 Low-Pressure Welded Vessel Specification at minimum
Implement program to assess pressure vessels against updated RAGAGEP at least once every five years
Develop and implement written procedures to document and maintain records of all inspections, alterations, and maintenance/repairs on all pressure vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service
Demonstrate use of prevention through design using hierarchy of controls in future resin plant designs, prioritizing inherently safer design and engineering controls
Identify and document all equipment that could release flammable materials and install LEL detectors in accordance with CCPS guidelines
Ensure detection of hazardous conditions automatically triggers both visual and audible alarms to alert plant personnel
Develop and implement employee training on actions to take when alarms are activated, such as prompt evacuation
Develop and implement requirements for personnel to wear flame-resistant uniforms in all operating areas that process flammable chemicals
Update employee training material to include requirement for and purpose of PPE use
API to develop specific design, construction, and alteration guidance for low-pressure process vessels in flammable and other highly hazardous chemicals service not exceeding 15 psig
ASME to assist API in developing design, construction, and alteration guidance for low-pressure vessels in flammable and other highly hazardous chemicals service not exceeding 15 psig
